Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and patch locations)

Root cause
- While formatting a failing repr() of a user object, pytest's saferepr code attempted to call instance-level operations that can invoke user code:
  - _format_repr_exception used obj.__class__.__name__ (this triggers instance attribute lookup and may call a broken __getattribute__ on the object).
  - _try_repr_or_str's fallback attempted to include obj in a format string, calling str(obj)/__format__ and thereby potentially invoking user code (broken __str__, __repr__, or __getattribute__).
- When the object has broken __getattribute__ or __repr__ (raising errors), these extra invocations cause more exceptions while pytest is already handling an exception, culminating in an INTERNALERROR.

All locations that need to change (complete fix)
1) File: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py
   - Change 1: _try_repr_or_str fallback — replace the use of the object (obj) in the fallback format with a safe fallback that does not call obj.__str__ or obj.__repr__:
     - Current: return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)
     - New: return "{} object at 0x{:x}".format(type(obj).__name__, id(obj)) (with a tiny additional fallback to just the type name if even that fails).
     - Why: avoids calling into user-defined __str__/__repr__ or __format__, which can raise or cause further problems.
   - Change 2: _format_repr_exception — avoid accessing obj.__class__.__name__:
     - Current: ... obj.__class__.__name__ ...
     - New: ... type(obj).__name__ ...
     - Why: obj.__class__ attribute access can be implemented via instance-level descriptors / __getattribute__ and thus call user code; type(obj) is guaranteed to return the object's type without invoking instance-level code and is safe.

Why these are the only locations necessary
- saferepr is the central place used when building safe representations of arbitrary user objects during exception formatting. The problem was specifically unsafe instance-level accesses performed by saferepr's fallback code. Changing these two spots prevents saferepr itself from triggering user code when it is trying to recover from a failing repr, eliminating the INTERNALERROR observed. Other uses of __class__.__name__ within pytest are typically used on pytest-internal objects (self = objects owned by pytest) and are not exposed to arbitrary user implementations; those are not strictly required to change for this bug fix.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- No numeric constants needed change. The only "hardcoded" format strings were the ones calling obj and obj.__class__.__name__, which were unsafe; replacing them with type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) avoids dynamic calls on the user object. The ellipsize arithmetic (uses 3 for "...") remains correct.

Methods to add/override
- None. All parent methods still apply; SafeRepr continues to override repr and repr_instance as before. No additional overrides needed.

Extra notes and rationale
- Using type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) is safe: neither invokes instance-level code on obj, and id(obj) is a built-in that just returns the memory address/value and does not involve attribute access. object.__repr__(obj) could be used as an alternative for a safe object repr, but the existing code historically returns "ClassName object at 0x..."; so using type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) preserves that style.
- The fix respects the existing public-facing strings (we keep the same outer angle-bracketed format), while making the exception formatting robust against objects with broken __getattribute__, __repr__, or __str__ methods.
- I reviewed tests in testing/io/test_saferepr.py to ensure behavior is preserved for the tested cases (the new fallback is only used when repr(exc) fails; in most test cases repr(exc) still succeeds; where it fails, returning a safe type-name+id is arguably better than invoking user code and risking INTERNALERROR). The new behavior is aligned with the intent: saferepr should never raise.

Proposed patch (ready-to-apply)
- File: src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py — apply the two small changes described above. (Diff provided in Checkpoint 4 earlier.)

If you want, I can:
- Produce an exact patch file / git diff that you can apply.
- Run through other code locations to propose further defensive hardenings (e.g., other places that might call obj.__class__.__name__ for arbitrary user objects), but the minimal safe fix is confined to saferepr and is sufficient to prevent the INTERNALERROR reported.

bug_report.Title: INTERNALERROR when a failing __repr__ / broken __getattribute__ leads to pytest internal crash
bug_report.Description: When an object's __repr__ or __getattribute__ raises (for example a bare raise in __repr__), pytest's saferepr code attempts to build a fallback representation but invokes the object again while formatting the fallback. This can raise a second exception during exception-report formatting and results in an INTERNALERROR instead of a safe test failure report.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_try_repr_or_str — incorrect assumption: the fallback may safely format the object. The code's except-block returns '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj) (in the source: return '{}("{}")'.format(type(obj).__name__, obj)), which inserts obj into the format call and therefore invokes obj.__format__/str(obj)/__str__. If the instance has a broken __str__/__repr__/__format__ or a broken __getattribute__, this will execute user code and can raise while pytest is already handling an exception.

2) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py::_format_repr_exception — incorrect assumption: accessing obj.__class__.__name__ is side-effect-free. The code constructs the fallback using obj.__class__.__name__ (in the source: ... obj.__class__.__name__ ...), which performs an instance attribute lookup and therefore may invoke a user-defined __getattribute__ or descriptors on the instance, running user code while formatting a fallback.

3) src/_pytest/_io/saferepr.py:SafeRepr.repr and SafeRepr.repr_instance — these methods catch exceptions from repr() and call the two helpers above to produce a fallback representation. Because the helpers call back into the object (via formatting the object or accessing obj.__class__.__name__), the saferepr pathway is not robust: an exception raised while formatting the fallback will escape the intended safe repr logic and can escalate a user exception into an INTERNALERROR. All three locations above encode the same root cause pattern (unsafe calls into the instance while formatting a fallback). For the hardcoded-value parts of this defect, safe, side-effect-free expressions are type(obj).__name__ and id(obj) (these obtain the object's class name and identity without invoking instance-level attribute lookup).
StepsToReproduce: Create a file with the following code:
class SomeClass:
def __getattribute__(self, attr):
raise
def __repr__(self):
raise
def test():
SomeClass().attr
Run pytest on that file (e.g. pytest -q).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: Pytest reports the original user exception as a test failure and uses a non-crashing, user-code-free fallback for object representations when repr() fails (a visible fallback based on the object's type name and id or similarly safe string). No INTERNALERROR should be raised while formatting the traceback.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Pytest raises an INTERNALERROR during test collection/reporting. The traceback shows a secondary exception (for example RuntimeError: 'No active exception to reraise') originating from the user's __repr__ or attribute access while pytest was building a representation of the object, causing pytest's exception-reporting to abort and print an INTERNALERROR block instead of a normal test failure report.

Now answer the required checkpoints 4a–4e in detail.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current assumption: traceback file paths should be shortened relative to the current process working directory (py.path.local()), which may have been changed by the test/fixture.
- New assumption (after fix): when available, use the original invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir) as the base for producing relative paths in tracebacks; only fall back to current working directory when no invocation dir is available.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The hardcoded use of py.path.local() in FormattedExcinfo._makepath — this explicitly uses current working directory as the base for bestrelpath. Under the new assumption, py.path.local() is not correct when tests change CWD; it must be replaced by a dynamic base (startpath) which is supplied by callers that know the invocation directory (session or config).
- Other uses of bestrelpath in the code base that already rely on startdir/rootdir:
  - Session._bestrelpathcache uses config.rootdir (already correct for node locations).
  - terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath(...) (already correct).
- Conclusion: the specific hardcoded value to change is the reliance on py.path.local() inside _makepath. It must be made dynamic (use startpath when provided).

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The reverse operation would be: editors/IDEs take the displayed relative path and join it with some directory to find the file (the editor's CWD or workspace). If pytest outputs relative path "test_path_error.py:29", editors typically resolve that relative to the project root (the directory where pytest was invoked). Previously, because pytest used current working directory, editors would attempt to open file at path starting from their own project root and might fail because pytest displayed it relative to some nested "ddd" directory. By changing FormattedExcinfo to use startpath=self.session.startdir, the reverse operation (editor resolving relative paths) will succeed: editors can join the displayed path with the known project root to find the file.
- Trace through the complementary methods and check whether we need to change any reading code:
  - Parent methods that perform the complementary operation: none in code.py. But Node.location uses session._node_location_to_relpath which uses config.rootdir. That continues to produce node locations relative to the repository root.
  - For every inherited method that performs complementary operations: we checked Node._repr_failure_py (caller) — we now pass startpath=self.session.startdir so the formatting uses startdir.
- Concretely: ExceptionInfo.getrepr previously used py.path.local().bestrelpath internally; we now let callers pass startpath (invocation dir). The reverse operation (editor interpreting displayed path) is fixed by making the displayed path relative to the invocation directory rather than the current working directory changed by test code. No other complementary operations need an override because getrepr is still available in other contexts and defaults to existing behavior if startpath is not supplied.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- FormattedExcinfo methods:
  - All methods remain unchanged except _makepath. We only added an optional startpath attribute and used it instead of py.path.local() when provided. This is backward compatible: when startpath is None, behavior is unchanged.
- ExceptionInfo.getrepr:
  - Signature gains a new optional parameter startpath which defaults to None. Existing callers without the new argument will continue to work (backwards compatible).
- Node._repr_failure_py:
  - Passing startpath=self.session.startdir is safe: session.startdir is a py.path.local and is already present on Session instances. No other logic is affected.
- Config.notify_exception:
  - Passing startpath=self.invocation_dir is safe: invocation_dir is a py.path.local already available on Config. No other logic is affected.
- Other methods calling getrepr without startpath continue to get the prior behavior (bestrelpath relative to current cwd). Those cases are generally early boot errors where we don't have an invocation dir available (or the current cwd might be desired), so leaving default is acceptable.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are required. We added a new optional parameter to ExceptionInfo.getrepr and a new attribute on FormattedExcinfo (startpath) to carry the base. Because the parameter is optional (defaults to None), no callers are forced to change. We updated the two key callers that do have the correct base available (Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception) so they pass startpath and formatting will use it. No additional override methods are necessary.

Edge cases and further considerations:
- The code still checks abspath flag in Node._repr_failure_py based on whether os.getcwd() is accessible. That logic remains unchanged. The only change is the base for path shortening when abspath is False and startpath is provided.
- The change is minimal, targeted, and backward compatible.
- This also harmonizes well with terminal.py's use of startdir when showing other path references — now tracebacks will be shown relative to the same invocation directory as terminal reporting.

- Change FormattedExcinfo._makepath to use self.startpath.bestrelpath(path) if self.startpath is set; else fallback to py.path.local().bestrelpath(path).

Why these three locations?
- FormattedExcinfo._makepath is the actual origin of the wrong base path (it used current cwd).
- Node._repr_failure_py is the appropriate caller to supply the invocation directory (session.startdir) to getrepr so tracebacks use a stable base unaffected by tests/fixtures.
- Config.notify_exception is another place that formats exceptions where invocation_dir is available; using it keeps internal error reporting consistent.

Why not only change _makepath to use Session.startdir directly?
- _makepath does not have access to Session or Config. It's best to keep FormattedExcinfo generic and accept a startpath from callers. Some call sites (early config parsing) don't have a Config/Session; default behavior should be preserved there, so we keep a fallback to py.path.local().

Backward compatibility:
- getrepr gains an optional parameter with default None — existing plugins and tests calling getrepr without startpath continue to work, keep previous fallback behavior.
- Terminal output and item.location still work and remain consistent: node.location uses session._node_location_to_relpath (rootdir) and tracebacks now use startdir (invocation_dir) — these are coherent with terminal reporting using startdir.bestrelpath.

Now: respond to each required Checkpoint 4 subquestion explicitly with reference to code read

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- The current code assumes it's correct to shorten traceback file paths relative to the process current working directory (py.path.local()) at formatting time; my fix changes that assumption to "when available, use the original pytest invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir) as the base for relative paths shown in tracebacks."

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The specific hardcoded value is the use of py.path.local() inside FormattedExcinfo._makepath (code.py line ~794 in this commit). py.path.local() is the process current working directory. If the assumption (that current CWD is the right base) is no longer true, this value is not correct and must be made dynamic; that's exactly why we add startpath to FormattedExcinfo and use startpath.bestrelpath instead.
- I searched for other occurrences of bestrelpath: main.Session._bestrelpathcache uses config.rootdir (which remains correct), and terminal.py uses self.startdir.bestrelpath (already correct). So no other hardcoded constants need to be changed.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The complementary operation is editors/IDEs taking the displayed relative path and resolving it to an actual file to open. They typically do so relative to the project root (invocation directory). Before the fix, pytest printed relative paths relative to the test-modified current working directory, so editors could not find the files. After the fix, Node._repr_failure_py passes session.startdir into excinfo.getrepr, so FormattedExcinfo._makepath will produce paths relative to invocation dir (startdir), which is what editors expect and so the reverse operation (editor resolving displayed relative path to actual file) succeeds.
- Concrete trace through parent's methods:
  - Parent method: Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...). Previously getrepr used current cwd via py.path.local() and produced string path = "../test_path_error.py". Editor could not resolve to the actual test file when opening from project root.
  - After change: Node._repr_failure_py provides startpath=self.session.startdir (a py.path.local pointing at invocation_dir), so FormattedExcinfo._makepath uses startpath.bestrelpath(path) and produces "test_path_error.py". Editor joining that with project root opens the correct file.
- We also updated config.notify_exception to pass self.invocation_dir so internal error reporting uses a stable base.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Yes.
  - ExceptionInfo.getrepr: signature extended with startpath optional parameter — old callers unaffected.
  - FormattedExcinfo methods: _makepath now optionally uses self.startpath; if startpath is None behavior is unchanged. No other methods that rely on _makepath are impacted.
  - Node._repr_failure_py now passes a new optional arg; the rest of the method is unchanged.
  - Config.notify_exception now passes invocation_dir; unchanged semantics otherwise.

I went through each method in FormattedExcinfo used in traceback formatting — they all still behave correctly because the only change is to the _makepath shortening base (it is now controlled by the new attribute).

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are needed. We added an optional parameter to getrepr and a new attribute on FormattedExcinfo, which are sufficient. Because default behavior remains unchanged if startpath is omitted, we do not need to add wrapper functions or extra interfaces elsewhere.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and exact fixes)

Root cause
- The traceback formatting (FormattedExcinfo._makepath) used the current working directory (via py.path.local().bestrelpath) as the base for making paths relative. Tests/fixtures that change the current working directory cause pytest to print file paths relative to that new directory, which is not what editors/IDEs expect (they assume paths are relative to the pytest invocation directory or are absolute). This produced the wrong string "../test_path_error.py:29" instead of "test_path_error.py:29".

Complete fix (all affected locations)
1) src/_pytest/_code/code.py (primary fix)
- Change ExceptionInfo.getrepr signature to accept an optional startpath parameter (py.path.local):
  - def getrepr(..., chain: bool = True, startpath: Optional[py.path.local] = None) -> ...
- Pass startpath into FormattedExcinfo on construction:
  - fmt = FormattedExcinfo(..., startpath=startpath)
- Add a new attribute to FormattedExcinfo:
  - startpath = attr.ib(default=None)
- Update FormattedExcinfo._makepath to use startpath.bestrelpath(path) if startpath is not None, else fallback to py.path.local().bestrelpath(path):
  - if self.startpath is not None:
        np = self.startpath.bestrelpath(path)
    else:
        np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
- Rationale: make the path-shortening base configurable; callers that know the real invocation directory can pass it; others will get the old behavior.

2) src/_pytest/nodes.py
- Update Node._repr_failure_py to pass startpath=self.session.startdir into excinfo.getrepr:
  - return excinfo.getrepr(..., truncate_locals=truncate_locals, startpath=self.session.startdir)
- Rationale: the Session has startdir == config.invocation_dir, which is the directory pytest was invoked from. Using that as the base for relative paths is the expected behavior for displayed file paths.

3) src/_pytest/config/__init__.py
- Update Config.notify_exception to pass startpath=self.invocation_dir when it calls excinfo.getrepr:
  - excrepr = excinfo.getrepr(..., style=style, startpath=self.invocation_dir)
- Rationale: keep internal error reporting consistent and usable by editors/tools.

Why this fixes the original bug
- With these changes, traceback entries are shortened relative to the pytest invocation directory when that directory is available, rather than the current working directory which can be changed by test code. This ensures that printed file paths remain valid for editors/IDEs which expect paths relative to the project root or absolute paths.

Compatibility and safety
- getrepr gains a new optional parameter with a default of None; code that calls getrepr without startpath will keep the previous fallback behavior (shortening relative to current working directory), so backward compatibility is maintained.
- Only call sites that can and should supply a stable base (Session/Config) are updated.

Further notes / possible follow-ups
- There might be additional callers of getrepr introduced elsewhere in plugins or tests; since the new parameter is optional the change is backwards compatible, but tests/plugins that care about the base path might prefer to pass a startpath if they have one.
- I intentionally did not change the logic that sets abspath in nodes.Node._repr_failure_py (it checks os.getcwd() availability). The new change only replaces the base used for shortening when abspath is False and a startpath is provided.

Summary of code-level diffs (recap)
- src/_pytest/_code/code.py:
  - Add `startpath` optional argument to ExceptionInfo.getrepr and pass it into FormattedExcinfo.
  - Add `startpath` attribute to FormattedExcinfo and use it in _makepath.
- src/_pytest/nodes.py:
  - Pass `startpath=self.session.startdir` when calling excinfo.getrepr in Node._repr_failure_py.
- src/_pytest/config/__init__.py:
  - Pass `startpath=self.invocation_dir` when calling excinfo.getrepr in Config.notify_exception.

Why this is a COMPLETE fix
- It touches every place that: (a) formats tracebacks, and (b) calls getrepr where session/config are present and know the invocation directory. The key bug is that _makepath used py.path.local() — this single change plus passing the correct startpath from the two primary call sites (Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception) makes traceback display stable with respect to the invocation dir for regular test failures and internal error reporting. No other files need to be changed to fix the reported failure.

If you want, I can:
- Produce an actual patch file (unified diff) you can apply,
- Run through a few example traces and show before/after formatted outputs,
- Or search for any other call sites of getrepr and suggest whether they should pass startpath too.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify the draft report's claims against the actual source code and check for other call sites that share the same root-cause pattern. For each tool call below I state why I call it and what I expected to learn; after the tool result I'll analyze what it returned.

1) Verify FormattedExcinfo._makepath (claim: it uses current cwd via py.path.local().bestrelpath)

- Why I read src/_pytest/_code/code.py:
  The draft claims that traceback formatting shortens file paths using py.path.local().bestrelpath(path). That functionality lives in the exception/traceback formatting module. I expected to find a bestrelpath call there.

- What I looked up (file context returned):
  File src/_pytest/_code/code.py around FormattedExcinfo._makepath:

    def _makepath(self, path):
        if not self.abspath:
            try:
                np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
            except OSError:
                return path
            if len(np) < len(str(path)):
                path = np
        return path

- Analysis:
  This exactly matches the draft's claim: FormattedExcinfo._makepath uses py.path.local().bestrelpath(path). py.path.local() without arguments is the process current working directory. Therefore traceback path shortening is currently based on the runtime current working directory (CWD) at format time. The draft's description that formatting is using the new working directory is supported by this line.

2) Verify Node._repr_failure_py (claim: it calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying a stable base)

- Why I read src/_pytest/nodes.py:
  The draft claims Node._repr_failure_py is a caller of excinfo.getrepr and does not supply an invocation-root base. I expected to confirm the exact getrepr call site and whether any base is passed.

- What I looked up (file context returned):
  File src/_pytest/nodes.py around _repr_failure_py:

    try:
        os.getcwd()
        abspath = False
    except OSError:
        abspath = True

    return excinfo.getrepr(
        funcargs=True,
        abspath=abspath,
        showlocals=self.config.getoption("showlocals", False),
        style=style,
        tbfilter=False,  # pruned already, or in --fulltrace mode.
        truncate_locals=truncate_locals,
    )

- Analysis:
  Node._repr_failure_py indeed calls excinfo.getrepr(...) and does not pass any "base path" or invocation-dir argument. Node has access to self.session.startdir (session.startdir is set from config.invocation_dir), so a stable invocation directory is available at this call site. Draft claim that the caller doesn't provide a stable base is accurate.

3) Verify Config.notify_exception (claim: it calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying the invocation directory)

- Why I read src/_pytest/config/__init__.py:
  The draft mentioned Config.notify_exception as another caller that doesn’t supply the invocation directory. I expected to confirm the call site.

- What I looked up (file context returned):
  File src/_pytest/config/__init__.py around notify_exception:

    if option and getattr(option, "fulltrace", False):
        style = "long"
    else:
        style = "native"
    excrepr = excinfo.getrepr(
        funcargs=True, showlocals=getattr(option, "showlocals", False), style=style
    )
    res = self.hook.pytest_internalerror(excrepr=excrepr, excinfo=excinfo)

- Analysis:
  Config.notify_exception does call excinfo.getrepr(...) and does not pass self.invocation_dir (or any start/base path). Because Config has invocation_dir (see invocation_dir property), the caller has access to a stable invocation directory but does not pass it. Draft claim is supported.

4) Search for other getrepr call sites (completeness / same root-cause pattern)

- Why I searched for "getrepr(":
  The pattern is "callers of ExceptionInfo.getrepr call it without a base path while they have access to a stable invocation directory". I needed to find all such call sites so the RootCause enumerates every location sharing the same root cause pattern.

- What I found (search results + file contexts):

  - src/_pytest/config/__init__.py (main, earlier in bootstrapping):
      exc_info.getrepr(style="short", chain=False)
    Context: This is in main() inside an except ConftestImportFailure handler, executed before config is prepared. In that site a config/session is not available, so no stable invocation_dir is available to pass; this call is not a missed opportunity (no invocation_dir accessible).

  - src/_pytest/python.py:
    - except SyntaxError:
        raise self.CollectError(ExceptionInfo.from_current().getrepr(style="short"))
    - except ImportError:
        exc_repr = (
            exc_info.getrepr(style="short")
            if exc_info.traceback
            else exc_info.exconly()
        )
    Context: These are inside the module importer/collector code where self.config is available (module/collector methods). They call getrepr(...) without passing self.config.invocation_dir. That means the same root-cause pattern (no stable base passed despite having one) exists here.

  - src/_pytest/terminal.py:
    - in pytest_keyboard_interrupt: self._keyboardinterrupt_memo = excinfo.getrepr(funcargs=True)
    Context: TerminalReporter instance has access to self.config and thus to self.config.invocation_dir; this call site also calls getrepr without passing a stable base.

- Analysis:
  There are multiple call sites (python.py and terminal.py) which call getrepr without a stable base while they do have access to a config/session invocation directory. The draft RootCause listed nodes.py and config.notify_exception and the code._makepath issue, but it did not list python.py and terminal.py. These share the same root-cause pattern and must be included.

5) RootCause verification and content adjustments

- The root cause asserted in the draft (shortened here) is:
  - FormattedExcinfo._makepath shortens using py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) (current CWD).
  - Callers that format exceptions (Node._repr_failure_py, Config.notify_exception) do not provide a stable base (invocation dir), so the formatter falls back to py.path.local() and produces relative paths based on changed CWD.
  - The proper base for display should be the pytest invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir), i.e. use startdir.bestrelpath(path).

- Confirmation from code:
  - The code for _makepath shows py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) (quote above). Confirmed.
  - Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception call getrepr without a base (quotes above). Confirmed.
  - Session has startdir = config.invocation_dir and terminal.py already uses startdir.bestrelpath elsewhere (terminal reporter uses self.startdir.bestrelpath in other places), so the intended correct base is available and in use in other parts of pytest. Confirmed (terminal.py contains methods that use config.cwd_relative_nodeid / startdir.bestrelpath for other display).

6) Claims in the draft not supported by the code or needing softening

- The draft's RootCause point 2 explicitly states "The caller has access to a stable base (self.session.startdir == config.invocation_dir) and therefore should supply it to the exception formatter so the formatter can shorten paths relative to the invocation directory." This explains the diagnosis (callers commonly have the required base). Saying "should supply it" reads prescriptive. The guidelines allow stating the correct value when diagnosing hardcoded constants, but instruct to avoid prescribing a fix. I will reword to emphasize diagnosis: "callers have access to a stable base (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir) but do not pass it; as a result the formatter uses the current working directory."

- The draft omitted python.py and terminal.py call sites that also match the pattern. I will add them in RootCause with equal weight.

7) Impact completeness check (trace dependent code paths)

- Node/Session: Node._repr_failure_py is a primary call site for formatting test failures; it has access to session.startdir.
- Config.notify_exception: used to format internal errors; has access to invocation_dir.
- Python importer/collector code (python.py): uses getrepr when raising collection errors; it has access to self.config. Those errors may occur after fixtures change CWD (or even during import), so they can be affected.
- TerminalReporter (terminal.py): stores keyboard interrupt repr (excinfo.getrepr) in a member that is shown later. It calls getrepr without passing config.invocation_dir; TerminalReporter has this config available.
- Repr of node.location / Item.location: those are computed via session._node_location_to_relpath which uses config.rootdir (session._bestrelpathcache(initialized with config.rootdir)). Those uses are already relative to project root and are not the cause here — but they show the inconsistency: item.location is correct relative to rootdir while traceback entries may be shortened relative to CWD. This mismatch is part of the observed problem.

8) Field discipline check

- I checked each draft field's intended content:
  - Description should describe the visible bug: it does.
  - RootCause should only describe incorrect assumptions and the locations that encode them (with exact wrong and correct values when hardcoded values are involved). It currently does that for some locations but omits other call sites that share the same pattern. I will update it to list all relevant locations and keep it diagnostic (stating the wrong value and the correct base).
  - ExpectedBehavior should describe user-visible correct behavior: the draft's text is OK.
  - ObservedBehavior likewise is fine.

Summary of verification findings

- Confirmed: FormattedExcinfo._makepath uses py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) (current CWD) — exact code quote provided.
- Confirmed: Node._repr_failure_py and Config.notify_exception call excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying a stable invocation base; both code quotes provided.
- Additional call sites found that share the same root cause pattern and must be included in RootCause:
  - src/_pytest/python.py (module import/collect exception formatting) calls getrepr without passing config.invocation_dir, even though self.config is available there.
  - src/_pytest/terminal.py (pytest_keyboard_interrupt) calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without passing a stable base even though self.config is available.

These additional locations must be included in the revised RootCause with equal weight to the others.

---

bug_report.Title: Traceback paths shown relative to changed cwd when fixture calls os.chdir (should be relative to invocation dir)
bug_report.Description: When a test or fixture changes the process current working directory (os.chdir / monkeypatch.chdir), pytest formats traceback file paths relative to that new working directory. This makes the displayed path point outside the project root (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29") and prevents editors/IDEs from resolving the file location. The path shortening for traceback entries is being performed relative to the runtime current working directory rather than the pytest invocation directory (the directory pytest was started from).
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_code/code.py::FormattedExcinfo._makepath (the traceback formatter) uses py.path.local().bestrelpath(path) as the base for shortening file paths when abspath is False. That call uses py.path.local() (the process current working directory) as the base for bestrelpath; as a result, when tests change CWD the shortened path is computed relative to that changed directory rather than the invocation directory. (Code: "np = py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)")

2) src/_pytest/nodes.py::Node._repr_failure_py calls excinfo.getrepr(...) to format test failure tracebacks but does not provide a stable base path. The Node object (via self.session) has access to a stable invocation directory (self.session.startdir, which is set from config.invocation_dir), but that base is not supplied to the exception formatter, so the formatter falls back to using the process CWD.

3) src/_pytest/config/__init__.py::Config.notify_exception similarly calls excinfo.getrepr(...) without supplying the invocation directory (self.invocation_dir) as a base. Because Config has invocation_dir available, not providing it results in internal error reports being shortened relative to the current CWD.

4) The same caller-pattern is present in other places that format exceptions and have access to config/session but do not pass a base path to the formatter:
   - src/_pytest/python.py: the module importer/collector exception paths call getrepr(...) (e.g. when raising CollectError on SyntaxError or ImportError) without passing the invocation directory, despite having self.config available.
   - src/_pytest/terminal.py: pytest_keyboard_interrupt stores excinfo.getrepr(funcargs=True) without passing the terminal reporter's config invocation dir.

5) Precise relationship preserved from the investigation: the path-shortening base used for display is currently py.path.local() (the current working directory). For stable, editor-resolvable display the base should be the pytest invocation directory (session.startdir / config.invocation_dir). In other words: currently the code performs
   py.path.local().bestrelpath(path)
   but the display logic must use the invocation directory as the base when available, i.e.
   invocation_dir.bestrelpath(path)
   (session.startdir is the runtime Session attribute for the invocation directory). If callers have no invocation directory available (early bootstrapping errors), the previous behavior can be used as a fallback.
StepsToReproduce: Create a test file test_path_error.py with a fixture that creates a subdirectory and chdirs into it (os.chdir or monkeypatch.chdir).
In the test, raise an assertion failure (e.g. assert False).
Run pytest from the project root (the invocation directory).

Observe the failure report: the traceback file path is shown relative to the new working directory (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29") rather than relative to the invocation directory ("test_path_error.py:29").
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a test fails, file paths in tracebacks are displayed relative to the pytest invocation directory (the directory pytest was started from) or absolute, so editors/IDEs can resolve the file location consistently regardless of tests changing the process current working directory.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: If a fixture or test changes the current working directory, pytest displays traceback file paths shortened relative to that changed working directory (e.g. "../test_path_error.py:29"), making the displayed path wrong relative to the invocation directory and preventing editors from jumping to the file.

""" support for skip/xfail functions and markers. """
from typing import Optional
from typing import Tuple

from _pytest.config import Config
from _pytest.config import hookimpl
from _pytest.config.argparsing import Parser
from _pytest.mark.evaluate import MarkEvaluator
from _pytest.nodes import Item
from _pytest.outcomes import fail
from _pytest.outcomes import skip
from _pytest.outcomes import xfail
from _pytest.python import Function
from _pytest.reports import BaseReport
from _pytest.runner import CallInfo
from _pytest.store import StoreKey


skipped_by_mark_key = StoreKey[bool]()
evalxfail_key = StoreKey[MarkEvaluator]()
unexpectedsuccess_key = StoreKey[str]()


def pytest_addoption(parser: Parser) -> None:
    group = parser.getgroup("general")
    group.addoption(
        "--runxfail",
        action="store_true",
        dest="runxfail",
        default=False,
        help="report the results of xfail tests as if they were not marked",
    )

    parser.addini(
        "xfail_strict",
        "default for the strict parameter of xfail "
        "markers when not given explicitly (default: False)",
        default=False,
        type="bool",
    )


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    if config.option.runxfail:
        # yay a hack
        import pytest

        old = pytest.xfail
        config._cleanup.append(lambda: setattr(pytest, "xfail", old))

        def nop(*args, **kwargs):
            pass

        nop.Exception = xfail.Exception  # type: ignore[attr-defined] # noqa: F821
        setattr(pytest, "xfail", nop)

    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "skip(reason=None): skip the given test function with an optional reason. "
        'Example: skip(reason="no way of currently testing this") skips the '
        "test.",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "skipif(condition): skip the given test function if eval(condition) "
        "results in a True value.  Evaluation happens within the "
        "module global context. Example: skipif('sys.platform == \"win32\"') "
        "skips the test if we are on the win32 platform. see "
        "https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/skipping.html",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "xfail(condition, reason=None, run=True, raises=None, strict=False): "
        "mark the test function as an expected failure if eval(condition) "
        "has a True value. Optionally specify a reason for better reporting "
        "and run=False if you don't even want to execute the test function. "
        "If only specific exception(s) are expected, you can list them in "
        "raises, and if the test fails in other ways, it will be reported as "
        "a true failure. See https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/skipping.html",
    )


@hookimpl(tryfirst=True)
def pytest_runtest_setup(item: Item) -> None:
    # Check if skip or skipif are specified as pytest marks
    item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = False
    eval_skipif = MarkEvaluator(item, "skipif")
    if eval_skipif.istrue():
        item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = True
        skip(eval_skipif.getexplanation())

    for skip_info in item.iter_markers(name="skip"):
        item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] = True
        if "reason" in skip_info.kwargs:
            skip(skip_info.kwargs["reason"])
        elif skip_info.args:
            skip(skip_info.args[0])
        else:
            skip("unconditional skip")

    item._store[evalxfail_key] = MarkEvaluator(item, "xfail")
    check_xfail_no_run(item)


@hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem: Function):
    check_xfail_no_run(pyfuncitem)
    outcome = yield
    passed = outcome.excinfo is None
    if passed:
        check_strict_xfail(pyfuncitem)


def check_xfail_no_run(item: Item) -> None:
    """check xfail(run=False)"""
    if not item.config.option.runxfail:
        evalxfail = item._store[evalxfail_key]
        if evalxfail.istrue():
            if not evalxfail.get("run", True):
                xfail("[NOTRUN] " + evalxfail.getexplanation())


def check_strict_xfail(pyfuncitem: Function) -> None:
    """check xfail(strict=True) for the given PASSING test"""
    evalxfail = pyfuncitem._store[evalxfail_key]
    if evalxfail.istrue():
        strict_default = pyfuncitem.config.getini("xfail_strict")
        is_strict_xfail = evalxfail.get("strict", strict_default)
        if is_strict_xfail:
            del pyfuncitem._store[evalxfail_key]
            explanation = evalxfail.getexplanation()
            fail("[XPASS(strict)] " + explanation, pytrace=False)


@hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
    outcome = yield
    rep = outcome.get_result()
    evalxfail = item._store.get(evalxfail_key, None)
    # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
    if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
        reason = item._store[unexpectedsuccess_key]
        if reason:
            rep.longrepr = "Unexpected success: {}".format(reason)
        else:
            rep.longrepr = "Unexpected success"
        rep.outcome = "failed"

    elif item.config.option.runxfail:
        pass  # don't interfere
    elif call.excinfo and isinstance(call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception):
        assert call.excinfo.value.msg is not None
        rep.wasxfail = "reason: " + call.excinfo.value.msg
        rep.outcome = "skipped"
    elif evalxfail and not rep.skipped and evalxfail.wasvalid() and evalxfail.istrue():
        if call.excinfo:
            if evalxfail.invalidraise(call.excinfo.value):
                rep.outcome = "failed"
            else:
                rep.outcome = "skipped"
                rep.wasxfail = evalxfail.getexplanation()
        elif call.when == "call":
            strict_default = item.config.getini("xfail_strict")
            is_strict_xfail = evalxfail.get("strict", strict_default)
            explanation = evalxfail.getexplanation()
            if is_strict_xfail:
                rep.outcome = "failed"
                rep.longrepr = "[XPASS(strict)] {}".format(explanation)
            else:
                rep.outcome = "passed"
                rep.wasxfail = explanation
    elif (
        item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True)
        and rep.skipped
        and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple
    ):
        # skipped by mark.skipif; change the location of the failure
        # to point to the item definition, otherwise it will display
        # the location of where the skip exception was raised within pytest
        _, _, reason = rep.longrepr
        filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]
        assert line is not None
        rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason


# called by terminalreporter progress reporting


def pytest_report_teststatus(report: BaseReport) -> Optional[Tuple[str, str, str]]:
    if hasattr(report, "wasxfail"):
        if report.skipped:
            return "xfailed", "x", "XFAIL"
        elif report.passed:
            return "xpassed", "X", "XPASS"
    return None

from typing import Any
from typing import cast
from typing import Dict
from typing import Generic
from typing import TypeVar
from typing import Union


__all__ = ["Store", "StoreKey"]


T = TypeVar("T")
D = TypeVar("D")


class StoreKey(Generic[T]):
    """StoreKey is an object used as a key to a Store.

    A StoreKey is associated with the type T of the value of the key.

    A StoreKey is unique and cannot conflict with another key.
    """

    __slots__ = ()


class Store:
    """Store is a type-safe heterogenous mutable mapping that
    allows keys and value types to be defined separately from
    where it (the Store) is created.

    Usually you will be given an object which has a ``Store``:

    .. code-block:: python

        store: Store = some_object.store

    If a module wants to store data in this Store, it creates StoreKeys
    for its keys (at the module level):

    .. code-block:: python

        some_str_key = StoreKey[str]()
        some_bool_key = StoreKey[bool]()

    To store information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # Value type must match the key.
        store[some_str_key] = "value"
        store[some_bool_key] = True

    To retrieve the information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # The static type of some_str is str.
        some_str = store[some_str_key]
        # The static type of some_bool is bool.
        some_bool = store[some_bool_key]

    Why use this?
    -------------

    Problem: module Internal defines an object. Module External, which
    module Internal doesn't know about, receives the object and wants to
    attach information to it, to be retrieved later given the object.

    Bad solution 1: Module External assigns private attributes directly on
    the object. This doesn't work well because the type checker doesn't
    know about these attributes and it complains about undefined attributes.

    Bad solution 2: module Internal adds a ``Dict[str, Any]`` attribute to
    the object. Module External stores its data in private keys of this dict.
    This doesn't work well because retrieved values are untyped.

    Good solution: module Internal adds a ``Store`` to the object. Module
    External mints StoreKeys for its own keys. Module External stores and
    retrieves its data using these keys.
    """

    __slots__ = ("_store",)

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store = {}  # type: Dict[StoreKey[Any], object]

    def __setitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T], value: T) -> None:
        """Set a value for key."""
        self._store[key] = value

    def __getitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> T:
        """Get the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        return cast(T, self._store[key])

    def get(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: D) -> Union[T, D]:
        """Get the value for key, or return default if the key wasn't set
        before."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            return default

    def setdefault(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: T) -> T:
        """Return the value of key if already set, otherwise set the value
        of key to default and return default."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            self[key] = default
            return default

    def __delitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> None:
        """Delete the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        del self._store[key]

    def __contains__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> bool:
        """Returns whether key was set."""
        return key in self._store

class Config:
    """
    Access to configuration values, pluginmanager and plugin hooks.

    :param PytestPluginManager pluginmanager:

    :param InvocationParams invocation_params:
        Object containing the parameters regarding the ``pytest.main``
        invocation.
    """

    @attr.s(frozen=True)
    class InvocationParams:
        """Holds parameters passed during ``pytest.main()``

        The object attributes are read-only.

        .. versionadded:: 5.1

        .. note::

            Note that the environment variable ``PYTEST_ADDOPTS`` and the ``addopts``
            ini option are handled by pytest, not being included in the ``args`` attribute.

            Plugins accessing ``InvocationParams`` must be aware of that.
        """

        args = attr.ib(converter=tuple)
        """tuple of command-line arguments as passed to ``pytest.main()``."""
        plugins = attr.ib()
        """list of extra plugins, might be `None`."""
        dir = attr.ib(type=Path)
        """directory where ``pytest.main()`` was invoked from."""

    def __init__(
        self,
        pluginmanager: PytestPluginManager,
        *,
        invocation_params: Optional[InvocationParams] = None
    ) -> None:
        from .argparsing import Parser, FILE_OR_DIR

        if invocation_params is None:
            invocation_params = self.InvocationParams(
                args=(), plugins=None, dir=Path.cwd()
            )

        self.option = argparse.Namespace()
        """access to command line option as attributes.

          :type: argparse.Namespace"""

        self.invocation_params = invocation_params

        _a = FILE_OR_DIR
        self._parser = Parser(
            usage="%(prog)s [options] [{}] [{}] [...]".format(_a, _a),
            processopt=self._processopt,
        )
        self.pluginmanager = pluginmanager
        """the plugin manager handles plugin registration and hook invocation.

          :type: PytestPluginManager"""

        self.trace = self.pluginmanager.trace.root.get("config")
        self.hook = self.pluginmanager.hook
        self._inicache = {}  # type: Dict[str, Any]
        self._override_ini = ()  # type: Sequence[str]
        self._opt2dest = {}  # type: Dict[str, str]
        self._cleanup = []  # type: List[Callable[[], None]]
        # A place where plugins can store information on the config for their
        # own use. Currently only intended for internal plugins.
        self._store = Store()
        self.pluginmanager.register(self, "pytestconfig")
        self._configured = False
        self.hook.pytest_addoption.call_historic(
            kwargs=dict(parser=self._parser, pluginmanager=self.pluginmanager)
        )

        if TYPE_CHECKING:
            from _pytest.cacheprovider import Cache

            self.cache = None  # type: Optional[Cache]

    @property
    def invocation_dir(self) -> py.path.local:
        """Backward compatibility"""
        return py.path.local(str(self.invocation_params.dir))

    def add_cleanup(self, func) -> None:
        """ Add a function to be called when the config object gets out of
        use (usually coninciding with pytest_unconfigure)."""
        self._cleanup.append(func)

    def _do_configure(self) -> None:
        assert not self._configured
        self._configured = True
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("default")
            self.hook.pytest_configure.call_historic(kwargs=dict(config=self))

    def _ensure_unconfigure(self) -> None:
        if self._configured:
            self._configured = False
            self.hook.pytest_unconfigure(config=self)
            self.hook.pytest_configure._call_history = []
        while self._cleanup:
            fin = self._cleanup.pop()
            fin()

    def get_terminal_writer(self):
        return self.pluginmanager.get_plugin("terminalreporter")._tw

    def pytest_cmdline_parse(
        self, pluginmanager: PytestPluginManager, args: List[str]
    ) -> object:
        try:
            self.parse(args)
        except UsageError:

            # Handle --version and --help here in a minimal fashion.
            # This gets done via helpconfig normally, but its
            # pytest_cmdline_main is not called in case of errors.
            if getattr(self.option, "version", False) or "--version" in args:
                from _pytest.helpconfig import showversion

                showversion(self)
            elif (
                getattr(self.option, "help", False) or "--help" in args or "-h" in args
            ):
                self._parser._getparser().print_help()
                sys.stdout.write(
                    "\nNOTE: displaying only minimal help due to UsageError.\n\n"
                )

            raise

        return self

    def notify_exception(
        self,
        excinfo: ExceptionInfo[BaseException],
        option: Optional[argparse.Namespace] = None,
    ) -> None:
        if option and getattr(option, "fulltrace", False):
            style = "long"  # type: _TracebackStyle
        else:
            style = "native"
        excrepr = excinfo.getrepr(
            funcargs=True, showlocals=getattr(option, "showlocals", False), style=style
        )
        res = self.hook.pytest_internalerror(excrepr=excrepr, excinfo=excinfo)
        if not any(res):
            for line in str(excrepr).split("\n"):
                sys.stderr.write("INTERNALERROR> %s\n" % line)
                sys.stderr.flush()

    def cwd_relative_nodeid(self, nodeid):
        # nodeid's are relative to the rootpath, compute relative to cwd
        if self.invocation_dir != self.rootdir:
            fullpath = self.rootdir.join(nodeid)
            nodeid = self.invocation_dir.bestrelpath(fullpath)
        return nodeid

    @classmethod
    def fromdictargs(cls, option_dict, args):
        """ constructor usable for subprocesses. """
        config = get_config(args)
        config.option.__dict__.update(option_dict)
        config.parse(args, addopts=False)
        for x in config.option.plugins:
            config.pluginmanager.consider_pluginarg(x)

    assert False

Checkpoint 1: Identify the bug

1a) classify_report result (entities extracted)
- Methods/functions mentioned: cached_eval, MarkEvaluator._istrue, pytest.mark.skipif, test_should_skip, test_should_not_skip
- Class mentioned: MarkEvaluator
- File mentioned: _pytest/mark/evaluate.py

1b) Files located in codebase (search results)
- src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py — defines cached_eval and class MarkEvaluator (lines referenced)
- src/_pytest/skipping.py — imports MarkEvaluator and uses it during test setup and reporting

1c) Read source of relevant candidates
- I opened src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (full file). Key code:
  - cached_eval(config, expr, d): uses config._store[evalcache_key] and caches by expr string only. It compiles and eval()s expr in dict d and stores the result keyed by expr.
  - class MarkEvaluator: _istrue() iterates marks, and when expr is a str, it constructs d = self._getglobals() (which includes module globals) and calls cached_eval(self.item.config, expr, d).
- I opened src/_pytest/skipping.py (full file). Key usages:
  - pytest_runtest_setup constructs MarkEvaluator(item, "skipif") and calls istrue(); skip() if true.
  - pytest_configure adds documentation that skipif condition evaluation happens within module global context.
  - check_xfail_no_run and other functions use MarkEvaluator similarly.

1d) Clear statement of the bug and primary responsible code
- Bug: The evaluation of string conditions in markers (skipif/xfail) depends on the module globals (the evaluation context), but the evaluation results are cached using only the expression string as the key (cached_eval stores result under evalcache[expr]). When two different modules define different globals (e.g., variable skip True in one, False in another) and both use the same string condition (e.g., "skip"), the cached result for the first evaluation is reused for the second, producing incorrect skipping/xfail behavior.
- Primary responsible code: cached_eval in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, and its use in MarkEvaluator._istrue (also in same file) — specifically, caching keyed only by the expression string without accounting for the globals (or item/module) leads to stale/wrong results.

Thought: I'll continue and finish the investigation per the required checkpoints (3–5). Below I give the tools I used and my reasoning before each call, then analyze results and produce a complete fix and justification.

Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

Reasoning before tool calls
- I need to inspect the files/classes that MarkEvaluator interacts with so I can list hardcoded values, component classes, and the parent class. I will re-open evaluate.py (already read) to ensure I have the primary file, then open the callers and component class files: skipping.py, nodes.py (Item/Node), store.py (StoreKey/Store), and mark structures (Mark) to check how marks and globals are provided.

Tool calls and results (with analysis)

1) get_file_context: evaluate.py (I already read this; done earlier)
- I inspected the entire file src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (lines 1–135). Relevant items:
  - Module-level evalcache_key = StoreKey[Dict[str, Any]]().
  - cached_eval(config, expr, d) — currently caches by expr string only:
    - evalcache = config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default)
    - tries evalcache[expr], otherwise compiles & evals expr in dict d and stores evalcache[expr] = result
  - class MarkEvaluator:
    - __init__(self, item, name)
    - _getglobals: returns a freshly constructed dict containing os, sys, platform, config, then updates it with self.item.obj.__globals__ if item has attribute 'obj'
    - _istrue iterates marks, and when expr is str does:
        d = self._getglobals()
        result = cached_eval(self.item.config, expr, d)
    - So MarkEvaluator calls cached_eval passing a new merged dict created each time.

Analysis:
- Primary bug candidate: cached_eval uses the expression string as the sole cache key, while the evaluation context (module globals) varies by module/item. This allows re-use of a cached result from a different module when expr is identical (e.g., both modules have marker "skip" condition "skip" but module-local variable differs).
- Also note: MarkEvaluator._getglobals constructs a new dict each time, so if we keyed on id(d) or on the dict object, that would defeat caching (new dict => new id each time). Any cache key must be stable across calls for the same module.

2) get_file_context: skipping.py
- I inspected src/_pytest/skipping.py (full file).
- MarkEvaluator is used by pytest_runtest_setup and other hooks (pytest_runtest_setup constructs MarkEvaluator(item, "skipif") and calls istrue(); pytest_runtest_setup stores MarkEvaluator(item, "xfail") in item._store). Behavior and reporting depend on MarkEvaluator. So cached_eval errors manifest as wrong skip/xfail decisions.

3) get_file_context: testing/test_skipping.py
- I inspected relevant tests. There are multiple tests that validate skip/xfail behavior and expectations that mark conditions are evaluated in module global context; some tests exercise multiple marks or multiple modules. The tests assume that the same string expression in different modules behaves according to each module's globals.

4) get_file_context: src/_pytest/store.py
- I inspected Store and StoreKey — Store is a mapping keyed by StoreKey objects; config._store is a Store() instance on Config. evalcache_key is a module-level StoreKey; config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default) stores the eval cache dict per config instance (per test session). No special behavior there.

5) get_file_context: src/_pytest/mark/structures.py
- I inspected Mark and MarkDecorator implementations to confirm mark.args, mark.kwargs usage and mark storage. Nothing surprising: markers are stored in node.own_markers and iterated by Node.iter_markers().

6) get_file_context: src/_pytest/nodes.py
- I inspected Node/Item. Node.listchain() and iter_markers() yield marks from function/class/module, using node.own_markers (module/class/function's own_markers). Node has attribute fspath and reportinfo for filename/line info.

What I learned (Checkpoint 3 conclusions)
- The core issue is in cached_eval: it uses expr string only as cache key.
- MarkEvaluator's _getglobals builds a fresh dict containing module globals (item.obj.__globals__). That merged dict will have keys "__name__" and "__file__" coming from the module globals.
- Using the merged dict itself as cache discriminator (via id(d)) would be unstable because a new dict is created on each call.
- The cache should partition by the evaluation context (typically the defining module), not just by the text of the expression. A stable identifier readily available in the merged dict is the module's "__file__" (source file path) and/or "__name__". These are present in the merged dict and are stable across the multiple calls for marks in the same module and distinct across modules.
- evalcache_key is only used in evaluate.py; no other code depends on the exact shape of the evalcache contents, so changing the cache key structure is safe in terms of other modules.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (summary)
- Change cached_eval to include the evaluation context (module identity) in the cache key so that identical expressions in different modules do not clash in the cache.
- Specifically, build the cache key as (expr, d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__")). Using __file__ (module filename) and __name__ (module name) gives a stable and distinctive context per module but does not depend on the dict object identity (which is new each call).
- This requires changing only cached_eval in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (single location where the cache key is formed).
- No change required to MarkEvaluator._getglobals or other callers.

Concrete patch for src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py (replace cached_eval implementation)
- Current implementation (lines ~21–31):
  def cached_eval(config: Config, expr: str, d: Dict[str, object]) -> Any:
      default = {}
      evalcache = config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default)
      try:
          return evalcache[expr]
      except KeyError:
          import _pytest._code

- New implementation:
  def cached_eval(config: Config, expr: str, d: Dict[str, object]) -> Any:

I will now answer the required challenge questions 4a–4e, referencing code lines and behavior.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence: The current code assumes that the boolean result of evaluating an expression depends only on the expression string itself (it encodes the assumption "expr text uniquely determines the result"), whereas my fix assumes the result also depends on the evaluation context (module identity), so cache entries must be partitioned by module context as well.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The primary hardcoded value encoding the incorrect assumption is the use of expr as the sole key in evalcache:
  - evaluate.py, cached_eval: evalcache[expr] — this is the code that encoded the assumption.
- Other "hardcoded" things observed:
  - MarkEvaluator._getglobals constructs the merged globals dict with specific entries:
      d = {"os": os, "sys": sys, "platform": platform, "config": self.item.config}
    This is not incorrect, and these keys are names inserted in the evaluation namespace. They do not encode the problematic assumption.
- Does any other component class have hardcoded constants that encode the assumption? No numeric offsets or other constants. So the only caching-related hardcoded key that must change is the expr-only key inside cached_eval.

Is the new key (expr, d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__")) still correct if assumption is removed?
- Yes. d.get("__file__") and d.get("__name__") are stable attributes of the module globals copied into d in MarkEvaluator._getglobals (since we update the merged dict with the module's __globals__), and they differentiate modules that share the same expression string. This makes cache entries module-scoped and avoids cross-module pollution.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- The "reverse operation" here would be code that reads from the same config._store cache or otherwise expects the cache to be keyed by expr only. I performed these checks:
  - Search for any other code reading evalcache_key: only evaluate.py uses evalcache_key (search results confirmed).
  - No other module writes into or reads from this cache with assumptions about its structure. That means changing the internal cache key shape is internal to evaluate.py and does not require changes elsewhere.
- Parent class inspection: MarkEvaluator doesn't inherit from another domain-specific parent with complementary read/write methods; it is a standalone class in evaluate.py (it inherits from object implicitly). So there's no parent method that must be re-examined for complementary operations. I re-read MarkEvaluator methods (istrue, _getglobals, _istrue) to ensure that any complementary method that constructs cache keys is not present — none exist.
- Concrete variable substitution trace for parent's complementary operations (none applicable):
  - Parent is object: no inherited caching methods to consider.
- Thus, no additional reverse-operation changes are needed.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- I checked all MarkEvaluator methods (from evaluate.py):
  - __bool__ uses _get_marks() directly — unaffected.
  - wasvalid() inspects self.exc — unaffected.
  - _get_marks() yields item.iter_markers(name) — unaffected.
  - invalidraise() uses get("raises") — unaffected.
  - _getglobals() builds d — unchanged.
  - _istrue():
    - It calls self._getglobals() and passes d to cached_eval.
    - Because cached_eval now uses d.get("__file__") and d.get("__name__"), and those keys come from the module's globals present in d when item has obj, caching will be module-specific and stable across repeated calls: the merged dict will contain the module's __file__ and __name__ values which are identical across calls; although the dict object is new each time, the cache key uses those stable string values, so caching will work correctly for repeated evaluations in the same module.
    - If the merged dict doesn't contain __file__/__name__ (e.g., no item.obj, marks that only have os/sys/platform/config), the context will be (None, None) and such marks are global/common; caching under (None, None) is acceptable (previous behavior also shared across configuration), and behavior remains deterministic.
  - get() and getexplanation() access attributes set by _istrue — unaffected.
- Conclusion: All existing methods still work correctly under the fix. The only change is that cached evaluation is now partitioned by evaluation context.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- I reviewed the parent class list (not applicable — parent is object). I searched for other modules that would need to be updated (no other code uses evalcache_key), and I examined MarkEvaluator's responsibilities.
- No new methods are required. The MarkEvaluator API remains unchanged and its calls into cached_eval continue to work.
- Rationale: Because cached_eval accesses context information from the d passed by _getglobals (which contains module information), we don't need to change MarkEvaluator's interface. No inherited logic is violated.

Checkpoint 5: Final report (complete analysis and patch)

Root cause
- cached_eval in src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py cached results using the expression string only:
    evalcache[expr] = eval(...)
  This implicitly assumed results of evaluation depend only on expr text and not on the evaluation context (module globals).
- MarkEvaluator passes a merged globals dictionary to cached_eval which contains module globals (function/class module __globals__). The merged dict is re-created every time, but it includes stable keys like "__file__" and "__name__".
- Because cached_eval keyed only by expr, identical expressions in different modules shared the same cached value (from the first evaluation), producing incorrect skip/xfail decisions when module-level variables differ between modules.

Every location that needs change
- Single location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py — function cached_eval
  - Current behavior: caches by expr only.
  - Problem: cache cross-contaminates results across different modules that have different globals and thus different evaluation results.
  - Fix: Key the cache by a tuple (expr, module identity) where module identity is derived from the evaluation globals d — specifically using d.get("__file__") and d.get("__name__").
  - New behavior: cache key = (expr, (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__"))) so two different modules with the same expr will have separate cache entries.

Why this is a complete fix
- There is exactly one caller of cached_eval (MarkEvaluator._istrue), and MarkEvaluator already provides module globals (via its merged dict) including __file__ and __name__. So changing cached_eval to partition the cache by those stable context values is sufficient.
- No other code depends on the exact shape of evalcache, and evalcache_key is private to evaluate.py; changing the inner mapping key from str to a tuple is internal to this module and safe.
- Using (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__")) avoids using the merged dict object's id (which is different each call) and provides stable per-module identity.

Other considerations / edge cases / alternatives considered
- Could have used id(d) to discriminate contexts — but MarkEvaluator constructs a new merged dict for each evaluation call, so id(d) would vary across calls and break caching (would produce no cache hits).
- Could have passed the raw module globals dict as a separate argument to cached_eval (e.g., module_globals = item.obj.__globals__), and use id(module_globals) as discriminator. This would also work and be robust. However it requires changing MarkEvaluator to expose that module dict explicitly. I preferred the simpler approach that requires changing only cached_eval and relies on existing merged dict data (__file__/__name__).
- Could have used only d.get("__file__") or only d.get("__name__"). I chose to use both (a tuple) for extra disambiguation: __file__ is the physical file path (most unique), __name__ is the module name (useful when __file__ may be missing).
- For cases where __file__ and __name__ are both None (very unusual, e.g., ephemeral contexts), different modules may collide on the same cache entry (None, None). That replicates the prior behavior in those unusual cases but the behavior in common cases (module-defined marks) is now correct. If absolute isolation for such cases were needed, we could fall back to including config or nodeid in the cache key, but there is no evidence this is needed and it's safer to keep the key lightweight.

Exact code change (diff)
- File: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py
- Replace cached_eval implementation with this:

def cached_eval(config: Config, expr: str, d: Dict[str, object]) -> Any:
    # Use a cache stored on the Config to avoid re-evaluating
    # identical expressions in the same module. Use both the expression text
    # and the evaluation context (module identity derived from the globals dict)
    # as the cache key so different modules don't share evaluation results.
    default = {}  # type: Dict[object, object]
    evalcache = config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default)
    # Use module identity from the globals dict: __file__ and __name__ are stable
    # across calls for the same module and distinguish different modules.
    context = (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__"))
    key = (expr, context)
    try:
        return evalcache[key]
    except KeyError:
        import _pytest._code
        exprcode = _pytest._code.compile(expr, mode="eval")
        evalcache[key] = x = eval(exprcode, d)
        return x

Why this change is minimal and safe
- Only one file is changed and only one function is altered.
- MarkEvaluator and all callers remain unchanged.
- No unit tests or other modules rely on the precise structure of this internal cache (search confirmed evalcache_key is only used here).
- The new key avoids the stale cross-module cache bug by making cache entries module-aware.

- Alternatively, implement the alternative variant (pass module_globals explicitly from MarkEvaluator and key by id(module_globals)), if you prefer that approach.

Results (code evidence and analysis)
1) cached_eval and MarkEvaluator: code excerpts and verification
- evaluate.py defines the eval cache key and cached_eval:
  - evalcache_key = StoreKey[Dict[str, Any]]()  (line 18)

      - on KeyError it compiles & evals and sets evalcache[expr] = x = eval(exprcode, d) (lines 29-31)
  - This confirms the draft's claim that the cache key is the expression string alone.

- MarkEvaluator builds a new globals dict and passes it to cached_eval:
  - def _getglobals(self) -> Dict[str, object]: (line 76)
    - d = {"os": os, "sys": sys, "platform": platform, "config": self.item.config} (line 77)

  - This confirms MarkEvaluator constructs a fresh merged dict for each evaluation and passes it to cached_eval.

2) Root cause verification
- The root problem reported — that cached_eval caches by expr only and thus ignores module-specific globals — is supported by the code:
  - cached_eval uses evalcache[expr] (lines 23–31); MarkEvaluator passes module globals (via _getglobals), so identical expr strings from different modules can be evaluated under different globals but will share the same evalcache entry keyed by expr.
- The draft lists three numbered items in RootCause. Each item maps to code:
  1) cached_eval caches by expr text alone — supported by evaluate.py lines 21–31.
  2) MarkEvaluator constructs a new globals dict (so caching by dict identity would be unstable) — supported by _getglobals (lines 76–80) and the call site (lines 99–101). The dict is re-created on each call (update of new dict).
  3) evalcache_key is module-level and used to store evalcache on Config._store — supported by evalcache_key (line 18) and use of config._store (line 23).

3) Claims in the draft that are not supported or that need softening
- The draft's RootCause recommends a specific cache key shape (expr plus module identity tuple) and says the cache "must be changed" to map (expr, module_identity) -> result. As this is diagnosing a wrong hardcoded key, stating the current wrong key (expr) and a candidate correct key is acceptable as diagnosis. However, wording that prescribes "must be changed" is prescriptive; I will rephrase to present it as the correct relationship and the concrete values to use for disambiguation (allowed by the guidelines for hardcoded-value diagnoses) rather than as a mandate.

The draft JSON itself did not include that prescription, but the earlier narrative did. I will remove prescriptive phrasing from the revised report and keep the content diagnostic.

4) Missing or additional findings from the investigation analysis that must appear in RootCause
- The investigation noted that MarkEvaluator._getglobals merges module globals that include stable identifiers such as "__file__" and "__name__". These values are suitable discriminators to partition the cache by module context. This should be included in RootCause as a concrete diagnostic fact.
- Edge case: if the merged globals dict does not contain "__file__" and "__name__" (for example when item has no obj or the globals don't include them), d.get("__file__") and d.get("__name__") will be None; that will yield a shared cache key of (expr, (None, None)) across those contexts, which matches previous behavior for truly global contexts. This diagnostic note should be present.

This means changing the internal mapping key shape is internal to evaluate.py and will not require updates elsewhere; this should be stated in the evidence section, but it is not a root-cause item.

5) Impact completeness check
- The cache is stored on config._store: config._store is associated with a Config instance (the test session configuration). Therefore cache entries are shared across all modules in the pytest run that use the same Config. This is consistent with the observed cross-module contamination. I will include this diagnostic fact.
