bug_report.Title: String-condition caching ignores module context: skipif/xfail evaluations cached by expr only
bug_report.Description: pytest caches the result of evaluating string conditions (e.g. @pytest.mark.skipif("skip")) using only the expression text as the cache key. Because the result depends on the evaluation globals (module-level variables), the cache can return a result computed in one module for the same expression used in another module, causing incorrect skip/xfail outcomes.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) cached_eval uses the expression text as the sole cache key — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, function cached_eval (lines 21–31). Current behavior: the code reads/writes evalcache[expr] (evalcache = config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default); return evalcache[expr]; evalcache[expr] = eval(...)). Incorrect assumption: the boolean result depends only on the expression string.

2) MarkEvaluator constructs a fresh globals dict for each evaluation and passes it to cached_eval — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, class MarkEvaluator, methods _getglobals and _istrue (lines 76–80 and 99–101). Observation: _getglobals builds a new dict and, when the test item has an object, updates it with the module's __globals__, which contains stable module identifiers such as "__file__" and "__name__". Caching keyed only by the expression text therefore allows reuse of a result computed under a different module's globals.

3) The eval cache is stored on the Config instance (config._store) under a module-level StoreKey — location: src/_pytest/mark/evaluate.py, evalcache_key (line 18) and its use of config._store.setdefault(evalcache_key, default) (line 23). Effect: cached entries are shared for the entire test session (same Config). This is why an evaluation performed for one module can be reused for another module in the same pytest run.

4) Correct relationship and stable discriminators (diagnosis of the wrong hardcoded key): because evaluation depends on the module-global context, the cache key must include a stable module identity in addition to the expression text. A concrete discriminator available in the merged globals dict is the module filename and module name (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__")). For example, a cache key of the form (expr, (d.get("__file__"), d.get("__name__"))) will separate identical expressions evaluated in different modules. Note: if __file__ and __name__ are absent, their values will be None and the cache entry will be shared under the (None, None) context (this reproduces previous behavior for truly global/no-module contexts).
StepsToReproduce: Run: pytest test_module_1.py test_module_2.py
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: test_should_skip is reported as skipped; test_should_not_skip is executed (not skipped).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: Both tests are reported as skipped (the cached result from the first evaluation is reused for the second module).

from typing import Any
from typing import cast
from typing import Dict
from typing import Generic
from typing import TypeVar
from typing import Union


__all__ = ["Store", "StoreKey"]


T = TypeVar("T")
D = TypeVar("D")


class StoreKey(Generic[T]):
    """StoreKey is an object used as a key to a Store.

    A StoreKey is associated with the type T of the value of the key.

    A StoreKey is unique and cannot conflict with another key.
    """

    __slots__ = ()


class Store:
    """Store is a type-safe heterogenous mutable mapping that
    allows keys and value types to be defined separately from
    where it (the Store) is created.

    Usually you will be given an object which has a ``Store``:

    .. code-block:: python

        store: Store = some_object.store

    If a module wants to store data in this Store, it creates StoreKeys
    for its keys (at the module level):

    .. code-block:: python

        some_str_key = StoreKey[str]()
        some_bool_key = StoreKey[bool]()

    To store information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # Value type must match the key.
        store[some_str_key] = "value"
        store[some_bool_key] = True

    To retrieve the information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # The static type of some_str is str.
        some_str = store[some_str_key]
        # The static type of some_bool is bool.
        some_bool = store[some_bool_key]

    Why use this?
    -------------

    Problem: module Internal defines an object. Module External, which
    module Internal doesn't know about, receives the object and wants to
    attach information to it, to be retrieved later given the object.

    Bad solution 1: Module External assigns private attributes directly on
    the object. This doesn't work well because the type checker doesn't
    know about these attributes and it complains about undefined attributes.

    Bad solution 2: module Internal adds a ``Dict[str, Any]`` attribute to
    the object. Module External stores its data in private keys of this dict.
    This doesn't work well because retrieved values are untyped.

    Good solution: module Internal adds a ``Store`` to the object. Module
    External mints StoreKeys for its own keys. Module External stores and
    retrieves its data using these keys.
    """

    __slots__ = ("_store",)

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store = {}  # type: Dict[StoreKey[Any], object]

    def __setitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T], value: T) -> None:
        """Set a value for key."""
        self._store[key] = value

    def __getitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> T:
        """Get the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        return cast(T, self._store[key])

    def get(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: D) -> Union[T, D]:
        """Get the value for key, or return default if the key wasn't set
        before."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            return default

    def setdefault(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: T) -> T:
        """Return the value of key if already set, otherwise set the value
        of key to default and return default."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            self[key] = default
            return default

    def __delitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> None:
        """Delete the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        del self._store[key]

    def __contains__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> bool:
        """Returns whether key was set."""
        return key in self._store

def getworkerinfoline(node):
    try:
        return node._workerinfocache
    except AttributeError:
        d = node.workerinfo
        ver = "%s.%s.%s" % d["version_info"][:3]

class TestReport(BaseReport):
    """ Basic test report object (also used for setup and teardown calls if
    they fail).
    """

    __test__ = False

    def __init__(
        self,
        nodeid: str,
        location: Tuple[str, Optional[int], str],
        keywords,
        outcome: "Literal['passed', 'failed', 'skipped']",
        longrepr,
        when: "Literal['setup', 'call', 'teardown']",
        sections: Iterable[Tuple[str, str]] = (),
        duration: float = 0,
        user_properties: Optional[Iterable[Tuple[str, object]]] = None,
        **extra
    ) -> None:
        #: normalized collection node id
        self.nodeid = nodeid

        #: a (filesystempath, lineno, domaininfo) tuple indicating the
        #: actual location of a test item - it might be different from the
        #: collected one e.g. if a method is inherited from a different module.
        self.location = location  # type: Tuple[str, Optional[int], str]

        #: a name -> value dictionary containing all keywords and
        #: markers associated with a test invocation.
        self.keywords = keywords

        #: test outcome, always one of "passed", "failed", "skipped".
        self.outcome = outcome

        #: None or a failure representation.
        self.longrepr = longrepr

        #: one of 'setup', 'call', 'teardown' to indicate runtest phase.
        self.when = when

        #: user properties is a list of tuples (name, value) that holds user
        #: defined properties of the test
        self.user_properties = list(user_properties or [])

        #: list of pairs ``(str, str)`` of extra information which needs to
        #: marshallable. Used by pytest to add captured text
        #: from ``stdout`` and ``stderr``, but may be used by other plugins
        #: to add arbitrary information to reports.
        self.sections = list(sections)

        #: time it took to run just the test
        self.duration = duration

        self.__dict__.update(extra)

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return "<{} {!r} when={!r} outcome={!r}>".format(
            self.__class__.__name__, self.nodeid, self.when, self.outcome
        )

    @classmethod
    def from_item_and_call(cls, item: Item, call: "CallInfo[None]") -> "TestReport":
        """
        Factory method to create and fill a TestReport with standard item and call info.
        """
        when = call.when
        # Remove "collect" from the Literal type -- only for collection calls.
        assert when != "collect"
        duration = call.duration
        keywords = {x: 1 for x in item.keywords}
        excinfo = call.excinfo
        sections = []
        if not call.excinfo:
            outcome = "passed"  # type: Literal["passed", "failed", "skipped"]
            # TODO: Improve this Any.
            longrepr = None  # type: Optional[Any]
        else:
            if not isinstance(excinfo, ExceptionInfo):
                outcome = "failed"
                longrepr = excinfo
            elif excinfo.errisinstance(skip.Exception):
                outcome = "skipped"
                r = excinfo._getreprcrash()
                longrepr = (str(r.path), r.lineno, r.message)
            else:
                outcome = "failed"
                if call.when == "call":
                    longrepr = item.repr_failure(excinfo)

                    longrepr = item._repr_failure_py(
                        excinfo, style=item.config.getoption("tbstyle", "auto")
                    )
        for rwhen, key, content in item._report_sections:
            sections.append(("Captured {} {}".format(key, rwhen), content))
        return cls(
            item.nodeid,
            item.location,
            keywords,
            outcome,
            longrepr,
            when,
            sections,
            duration,
            user_properties=item.user_properties,
        )


class CollectReport(BaseReport):
    """Collection report object."""

    when = "collect"

    def __init__(

        nodeid: str,
        outcome: "Literal['passed', 'skipped', 'failed']",
        longrepr,
        result: Optional[List[Union[Item, Collector]]],
        sections: Iterable[Tuple[str, str]] = (),
        **extra
    ) -> None:
        #: normalized collection node id
        self.nodeid = nodeid

        #: test outcome, always one of "passed", "failed", "skipped".
        self.outcome = outcome

        #: None or a failure representation.
        self.longrepr = longrepr

        #: The collected items and collection nodes.
        self.result = result or []

        #: marshallable. Used by pytest to add captured text
        #: from ``stdout`` and ``stderr``, but may be used by other plugins
        #: to add arbitrary information to reports.
        self.sections = list(sections)

        self.__dict__.update(extra)

    @property
    def location(self):
        return (self.fspath, None, self.fspath)

    def __repr__(self) -> str:
        return "<CollectReport {!r} lenresult={} outcome={!r}>".format(
            self.nodeid, len(self.result), self.outcome
        )


class CollectErrorRepr(TerminalRepr):

    report: Union[CollectReport, TestReport]
) -> Optional[Dict[str, Any]]:

        data["$report_type"] = report.__class__.__name__
        return data
    return None

  - No magic offsets or numbers; no assumptions relevant to the bug.
- src/_pytest/reports.py: TestReport.from_item_and_call sets longrepr to (str(r.path), r.lineno, r.message) for skip exceptions (see runner.py and reports.py interplay). There is a final adjustment in skipping.py that adds +1 to the line when rewriting: rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason. That "line + 1" is a hardcoded numeric adjustment to map the reprcrash lineno to the test definition line.

  What the +1 represents: TestReport.from_item_and_call when building a longrepr for a skip uses excinfo._getreprcrash() (which grabs the location of the exception raise — typically inside pytest.skip call). The skipping.py rewrite uses item.reportinfo()[:2] which returns (filename, lineno) pointing to the item's location; adding 1 likely adjusts for the domain offset (to map to the function line inside a file). This +1 was present already and is unchanged by my fix; it's unrelated to runxfail short-circuiting, so it remains valid.

3b) Parent of the primary function
- The default implementation of pytest_runtest_makereport lives in src/_pytest/runner.py; it calls TestReport.from_item_and_call(item, call) and returns that. skipping.py registers a hookwrapper to receive and modify the report after it is created.

  Methods in runner.py relevant:
  - pytest_runtest_makereport(item, call) — returns TestReport.from_item_and_call(item, call)
  - call_and_report -> uses ihook.pytest_runtest_makereport to get a report and then calls pytest_runtest_logreport etc.
  - CallInfo and TestReport implementations — used to create the rep.

  Which of these does skipping.py override?
  - skipping.py provides a hookimpl(hookwrapper=True) for pytest_runtest_makereport, i.e., it wraps the report created by runner.py. It does not override the runner default function (runner.py defines a non-plugin default but plugin hooks get called).

3c) Sibling hook implementations
- src/_pytest/unittest.py also implements pytest_runtest_makereport (two hooks: tryfirst normal, and hookwrapper for other test types). That file performs special substitutions (e.g., translates unittest.SkipTest into pytest.skip) and does not contain the runxfail short-circuiting bug. It uses a different approach and does not interfere with skip location rewriting.

3d) Call graph (brief)
- runtestprotocol -> call_and_report -> call_runtest_hook -> (setup/call/teardown) -> runner.call_and_report -> runner.py calls pytest_runtest_makereport hook, which yields a TestReport (default created by runner.py), then skipping.py's hookwrapper receives it and updates rep attributes (wasxfail, outcome, longrepr adjustments). TerminalReporter then picks up report for output.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (concise)
- Problem: In pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py) there is an elif branch:

    elif item.config.option.runxfail:
        pass  # don't interfere

  placed before xfail-handling code and the final skip-location adjustment; because it is an elif in the same chain this prevents the skip-location adjustment from running when --runxfail is set.

- Minimal, correct change: do not short-circuit the whole chain when runxfail is True; instead only avoid executing the xfail-specific transformations in runxfail mode. Concretely: remove the early "elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass" branch and instead guard the xfail-specific branches with "and not item.config.option.runxfail".

Patch (exact edits to src/_pytest/skipping.py)
- Replace these lines (current code around lines ~269–285; exact):

    elif item.config.option.runxfail:
        pass  # don't interfere
    elif call.excinfo and isinstance(call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception):
        assert call.excinfo.value.msg is not None
        rep.wasxfail = "reason: " + call.excinfo.value.msg
        rep.outcome = "skipped"
    elif not rep.skipped and xfailed:
        if call.excinfo:
            raises = xfailed.raises
            if raises is not None and not isinstance(call.excinfo.value, raises):
                rep.outcome = "failed"
            else:
                rep.outcome = "skipped"
                rep.wasxfail = xfailed.reason
        elif call.when == "call":
            if xfailed.strict:
                rep.outcome = "failed"
                rep.longrepr = "[XPASS(strict)] " + xfailed.reason
            else:
                rep.outcome = "passed"
                rep.wasxfail = xfailed.reason

- With this edited version:

    elif call.excinfo and not item.config.option.runxfail and isinstance(
        call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception
    ):
        assert call.excinfo.value.msg is not None
        rep.wasxfail = "reason: " + call.excinfo.value.msg
        rep.outcome = "skipped"
    elif not item.config.option.runxfail and not rep.skipped and xfailed:
        if call.excinfo:
            raises = xfailed.raises
            if raises is not None and not isinstance(call.excinfo.value, raises):
                rep.outcome = "failed"
            else:
                rep.outcome = "skipped"
                rep.wasxfail = xfailed.reason
        elif call.when == "call":
            if xfailed.strict:
                rep.outcome = "failed"
                rep.longrepr = "[XPASS(strict)] " + xfailed.reason
            else:
                rep.outcome = "passed"
                rep.wasxfail = xfailed.reason

- No other changes in file needed.

Rationale: this keeps the xfail-specific logic from running when runxfail is set, but allows the later final branch that rewrites the skip longrepr into (item.file, item.line+1, reason) to run even when runxfail is True. That fixes the bug where skip location was left pointing to skipping.py when --runxfail was passed.

Now answer the required questions (4a–4e) in detail, referencing the code read above.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- Current code assumes that when the config option runxfail is True, the pytest_runtest_makereport hook should not run any of its report-transformation logic at all (the 'elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass' short-circuits the rest of the chain). The fix changes that assumption to: when runxfail is True we should skip only the xfail-specific transformations, but still run other transformations (notably the skip-location rewrite).

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- The main "assumption-encoding" element was the presence of the branch 'elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass' — not a numeric constant but a control-flow hardcoding. It prevented later code from executing via the elif chain structure.
- Hardcoded numeric value(s) in vicinity: the expression line + 1 in the skip-location rewrite (rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason). This encodes an offset assumption (that the reported line from item.reportinfo() should be incremented by 1 to get the right display line). The fix does not change that numeric; it's independent of the runxfail short-circuit. I verified that enabling runxfail only prevented the rewrite entirely; when the rewrite executes, line + 1 is the same adjustment as prior behavior and is still correct.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- Complementary (reverse) operations to consider:
  - The default runner.py pytest_runtest_makereport returns TestReport.from_item_and_call which constructs longrepr from the CallInfo; for skip exceptions TestReport.from_item_and_call constructs longrepr = (str(r.path), r.lineno, r.message). The skipping.py hook then sometimes rewrites rep.longrepr to point to item.reportinfo() instead of the internal skip call location.
  - When I change skipping.py to only guard the xfail-specific branches (instead of short-circuiting everything), I must ensure that overriding logic in skipping.py still properly handles the call.excinfo case where call.excinfo is an xfail.Exception or where xfailed is set — but only when runxfail is False. I implemented guards "and not item.config.option.runxfail" on the xfail branches, so those transformations will not run when runxfail True (which is intended).
  - Trace through parent's method (runner.py pytest_runtest_makereport => TestReport.from_item_and_call):
    - Variables for a skip-by-mark scenario:
      * call.excinfo: will be an ExceptionInfo wrapping skip.Exception raised by skipping.py.skip() in pytest_runtest_setup.
      * TestReport.from_item_and_call produces rep.when (probably "setup"), rep.outcome == "skipped", rep.longrepr = (path_of_skip, lineno_of_skip_call, message)
      * item._store[skipped_by_mark_key] is True (set in pytest_runtest_setup)
    - Under current (buggy) code when runxfail True:
      * skipping.py hits 'elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass' and thus does not reach the final branch to rewrite rep.longrepr. So rep.longrepr remains (skipping.py, lineno_of_skip_call, message) — bad.
    - Under my fix:
      * The xfail-specific branches are skipped (they have 'and not item.config.option.runxfail'), but the final 'skipped_by_mark_key' rewrite is still reached, so rep.longrepr is set to (filename, line+1, reason) where filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]. That matches pre-existing intended behavior (when runxfail False). Thus the reverse operation (default generation of rep.longrepr) is still adjusted correctly by skipping.py, and no new complementary operation is needed.
    - Concrete substitution example (values):
      * item.reportinfo() -> (test_it.py, 2, <domain string>)
      * r from excinfo._getreprcrash -> reprcrash pointing to skipping.py line 238
      * Before fix with --runxfail: rep.longrepr remains (src/_pytest/skipping.py, 238, "unconditional skip")
      * After fix with --runxfail: code reaches rewrite and sets rep.longrepr to ('test_it.py', 3, 'unconditional skip') (line+1). This is consistent with the original expected output.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods in skipping.py:
  - pytest_addoption: unaffected.
  - pytest_configure: unaffected.
  - evaluate_condition/evaluate_skip_marks/evaluate_xfail_marks: unaffected.
  - pytest_runtest_setup: unaffected (still sets skipped_by_mark_key True and raises skip()).
  - pytest_runtest_call: unaffected.
  - pytest_runtest_makereport: modified only to avoid a control-flow short-circuit; the xfail-specific logic still runs when runxfail is False; skip-location rewrite still runs always when the conditions match. All paths are preserved except the buggy behavior is fixed. No other method depends on the short-circuit.
  - pytest_report_teststatus: unaffected.
- Therefore all existing methods should still behave correctly under the fix.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new method is required. The fix is a small, local control-flow change in pytest_runtest_makereport to ensure the skip-location rewrite executes even when --runxfail is set.
- I re-examined the parent's methods (runner.py default makereport implementation, TestReport.from_item_and_call) and found no other complementary operation that needs an override.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (complete analysis + locations to change)

Root cause (short)
- An early "elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass" clause in the elseif chain in pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py) short-circuits the rest of the chain, preventing the code that rewrites skip-reports' longrepr to point at the test item from running when --runxfail is set. That causes skip location to point to pytest's own skipping.py instead of the test file.

Every location that needs to change (complete list)
- One location: src/_pytest/skipping.py — function pytest_runtest_makereport. (No other files need edits.)

For that location: current code, assumption it encodes, and the fix

- Current code (excerpt, simplified):

    elif item.config.option.runxfail:
        pass  # don't interfere
    elif call.excinfo and isinstance(call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception):
        ...
    elif not rep.skipped and xfailed:
        ...
    elif (item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True)
          and rep.skipped
          and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple):
        # rewrite longrepr to item location
        _, _, reason = rep.longrepr
        filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]
        rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason

- Assumption being encoded: "if runxfail is True, do not run any of this hook's transformations", i.e., short-circuit all xfail-related and skip-related transformations).

- Why it's wrong: --runxfail is specifically about how xfail-marked tests are treated; it should not affect skip-mark behavior. The skip-location rewrite is unrelated to xfail and must still be applied when a test was skipped due to mark.skip/mark.skipif. Because the code used an elif chain with the early runxfail branch, the skip rewrite never runs when runxfail True.

- The fix: adjust control flow so that only the xfail-specific branches are guarded by runxfail, not the entire chain. Implementation option chosen:
  - Remove the early `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` branch.
  - Add `and not item.config.option.runxfail` to the two xfail-related branches, i.e. make them:

        elif call.excinfo and not item.config.option.runxfail and isinstance(
            call.excinfo.value, xfail.Exception
        ):
            ...
        elif not item.config.option.runxfail and not rep.skipped and xfailed:
            ...

  This preserves the existing behavior for xfail handling when runxfail is False, but allows the final skip-location rewrite to run even when runxfail True.

Hardcoded values that need review
- line + 1 in the skip-location rewrite: this is an offset adjustment used to produce the display location; it remains appropriate and is unrelated to the runxfail short-circuiting. No change needed there.
- There were no other numeric constants or hardcoded positions that depend on the runxfail assumption.

Tests and expectations
- Existing behavior before change:
  - pytest -rs shows: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip
  - pytest -rs --runxfail shows: SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:238: unconditional skip (broken)
- With fix:
  - pytest -rs: unaffected
  - pytest -rs --runxfail: now shows SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip (as expected)

Why this fix is complete
- The only faulty control-flow was the early runxfail branch in the single hook function. No other modules take the same mistaken approach.
- The change is minimal: it only restricts runxfail's influence to xfail-specific transformations; skip-specific rewrite remains executed.
- I inspected the default report creation (runner.py/TestReport.from_item_and_call) and ensured the rewrite still does the correct thing by replacing the excinfo-based file/line with item.reportinfo() file/line. The added condition guards do not change that behavior.

Notes / final reasoning
- The bug was a pure control-flow short-circuit caused by a misplaced runxfail guard inside an elif chain. The fix reorders/guards the two xfail-related branches explicitly by checking not item.config.option.runxfail on each, allowing the later skip-location rewrite to run regardless of runxfail.
- This is a minimal, safe change with low surface area and is consistent with the intent of --runxfail (only alter xfail behavior).
- I reviewed related code (runner.py TestReport.from_item_and_call, store.py StoreKey, unittest.py hook) to ensure no other module needs adjusting.

- Add a small regression test to the test suite that checks skip location with and without --runxfail (recommended).

bug_report.Title: pytest.mark.skip location reporting broken when --runxfail is used
bug_report.Description: Tests skipped via @pytest.mark.skip / skipif show the skip location as pytest's internal skipping.py (the place where pytest.skip() is called) when pytest is run with --runxfail; without --runxfail the skip location correctly points to the test item.
bug_report.RootCause:
In src/_pytest/skipping.py, the pytest_runtest_makereport hookwrapper uses a single if/elif/... chain that contains an early branch checking `item.config.option.runxfail`. Concretely, the branch `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` (skipping.py lines 273–274) is evaluated before the later branches that handle xfail and the final branch which rewrites skip report locations. Because the runxfail check is an `elif` in the same chain, when `--runxfail` is set that branch is taken and the subsequent branch that performs the skip-location rewrite is never reached.

The location-rewriting branch that does not run under `--runxfail` is the following condition and rewrite (skipping.py lines 294–305):
- condition: `item._store.get(skipped_by_mark_key, True) and rep.skipped and type(rep.longrepr) is tuple`
- rewrite: `_, _, reason = rep.longrepr; filename, line = item.reportinfo()[:2]; rep.longrepr = str(filename), line + 1, reason`

This short-circuited control-flow (an overbroad `runxfail` guard placed as an `elif` in the chain) causes the report's longrepr to remain the exception-origin location (where pytest.skip() was raised inside skipping.py) instead of being rewritten to the test item's file/line. The single location that encodes the defect is src/_pytest/skipping.py in the pytest_runtest_makereport hook (the `elif item.config.option.runxfail: pass` branch and the surrounding xfail/skip handling branches).
StepsToReproduce:
Run pytest with the normal skip status output: pytest -rs
  -> observe: SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip

Run pytest with --runxfail: pytest -rs --runxfail
  -> observe: SKIPPED [1] src/_pytest/skipping.py:238: unconditional skip (location points into pytest internals instead of test file)
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When a test is skipped by @pytest.mark.skip / skipif the test-reported skip location should always point to the test item (file and line of the test), e.g. 'SKIPPED [1] test_it.py:3: unconditional skip', regardless of whether pytest was invoked with --runxfail.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: When pytest is run with --runxfail, the skip report shows the location of pytest.skip() inside pytest's internals (src/_pytest/skipping.py) instead of the test file/line. Without --runxfail the location is correct.

# down to the lower scopes such as to minimize number of "high scope"
# setups and teardowns


def reorder_items(items: "Sequence[nodes.Item]") -> "List[nodes.Item]":
    argkeys_cache = {}  # type: Dict[int, Dict[nodes.Item, Dict[_Key, None]]]
    items_by_argkey = {}  # type: Dict[int, Dict[_Key, Deque[nodes.Item]]]
    for scopenum in range(0, scopenum_function):
        d = {}  # type: Dict[nodes.Item, Dict[_Key, None]]
        argkeys_cache[scopenum] = d
        item_d = defaultdict(deque)  # type: Dict[_Key, Deque[nodes.Item]]
        items_by_argkey[scopenum] = item_d
        for item in items:
            # cast is a workaround for https://github.com/python/typeshed/issues/3800.
            keys = cast(
                "Dict[_Key, None]",
                order_preserving_dict.fromkeys(
                    get_parametrized_fixture_keys(item, scopenum), None
                ),
            )
            if keys:
                d[item] = keys
                for key in keys:
                    item_d[key].append(item)
    # cast is a workaround for https://github.com/python/typeshed/issues/3800.
    items_dict = cast(
        "Dict[nodes.Item, None]", order_preserving_dict.fromkeys(items, None)
    )
    return list(reorder_items_atscope(items_dict, argkeys_cache, items_by_argkey, 0))


def fix_cache_order(
    item: "nodes.Item",
    argkeys_cache: "Dict[int, Dict[nodes.Item, Dict[_Key, None]]]",
    items_by_argkey: "Dict[int, Dict[_Key, Deque[nodes.Item]]]",
) -> None:
    for scopenum in range(0, scopenum_function):
        for key in argkeys_cache[scopenum].get(item, []):
            items_by_argkey[scopenum][key].appendleft(item)


def reorder_items_atscope(
    items: "Dict[nodes.Item, None]",
    argkeys_cache: "Dict[int, Dict[nodes.Item, Dict[_Key, None]]]",
    items_by_argkey: "Dict[int, Dict[_Key, Deque[nodes.Item]]]",
    scopenum: int,
) -> "Dict[nodes.Item, None]":
    if scopenum >= scopenum_function or len(items) < 3:
        return items
    ignore = set()  # type: Set[Optional[_Key]]
    items_deque = deque(items)
    items_done = order_preserving_dict()  # type: Dict[nodes.Item, None]
    scoped_items_by_argkey = items_by_argkey[scopenum]
    scoped_argkeys_cache = argkeys_cache[scopenum]
    while items_deque:
        no_argkey_group = order_preserving_dict()  # type: Dict[nodes.Item, None]
        slicing_argkey = None
        while items_deque:
            item = items_deque.popleft()
            if item in items_done or item in no_argkey_group:
                continue
            argkeys = order_preserving_dict.fromkeys(
                (k for k in scoped_argkeys_cache.get(item, []) if k not in ignore), None
            )
            if not argkeys:
                no_argkey_group[item] = None
            else:
                slicing_argkey, _ = argkeys.popitem()
                # we don't have to remove relevant items from later in the deque because they'll just be ignored
                matching_items = [
                    i for i in scoped_items_by_argkey[slicing_argkey] if i in items
                ]
                for i in reversed(matching_items):
                    fix_cache_order(i, argkeys_cache, items_by_argkey)
                    items_deque.appendleft(i)
                break
        if no_argkey_group:
            no_argkey_group = reorder_items_atscope(
                no_argkey_group, argkeys_cache, items_by_argkey, scopenum + 1
            )
            for item in no_argkey_group:
                items_done[item] = None
        ignore.add(slicing_argkey)
    return items_done


def fillfixtures(function: "Function") -> None:
    """ fill missing funcargs for a test function. """
    warnings.warn(FILLFUNCARGS, stacklevel=2)
    try:
        request = function._request
    except AttributeError:
        # XXX this special code path is only expected to execute
        # with the oejskit plugin.  It uses classes with funcargs
        # and we thus have to work a bit to allow this.
        fm = function.session._fixturemanager
        assert function.parent is not None
        fi = fm.getfixtureinfo(function.parent, function.obj, None)
        function._fixtureinfo = fi
        request = function._request = FixtureRequest(function)
        request._fillfixtures()
        # prune out funcargs for jstests
        newfuncargs = {}
        for name in fi.argnames:
            newfuncargs[name] = function.funcargs[name]
        function.funcargs = newfuncargs
    else:
        request._fillfixtures()


def get_direct_param_fixture_func(request):
    return request.param


@attr.s(slots=True)
class FuncFixtureInfo:
    # original function argument names
    argnames = attr.ib(type=Tuple[str, ...])
    # argnames that function immediately requires. These include argnames +
    # fixture names specified via usefixtures and via autouse=True in fixture
    # definitions.
    initialnames = attr.ib(type=Tuple[str, ...])
    names_closure = attr.ib(type=List[str])
    name2fixturedefs = attr.ib(type=Dict[str, Sequence["FixtureDef"]])

    def prune_dependency_tree(self) -> None:
        """Recompute names_closure from initialnames and name2fixturedefs

        Can only reduce names_closure, which means that the new closure will
        always be a subset of the old one. The order is preserved.

        This method is needed because direct parametrization may shadow some
        of the fixtures that were included in the originally built dependency
        tree. In this way the dependency tree can get pruned, and the closure
        of argnames may get reduced.
        """
        closure = set()  # type: Set[str]
        working_set = set(self.initialnames)
        while working_set:
            argname = working_set.pop()
            # argname may be smth not included in the original names_closure,
            # in which case we ignore it. This currently happens with pseudo
            # FixtureDefs which wrap 'get_direct_param_fixture_func(request)'.
            # So they introduce the new dependency 'request' which might have
            # been missing in the original tree (closure).
            if argname not in closure and argname in self.names_closure:
                closure.add(argname)
                if argname in self.name2fixturedefs:
                    working_set.update(self.name2fixturedefs[argname][-1].argnames)

        self.names_closure[:] = sorted(closure, key=self.names_closure.index)

from typing import Any
from typing import cast
from typing import Dict
from typing import Generic
from typing import TypeVar
from typing import Union


__all__ = ["Store", "StoreKey"]


T = TypeVar("T")
D = TypeVar("D")


class StoreKey(Generic[T]):
    """StoreKey is an object used as a key to a Store.

    A StoreKey is associated with the type T of the value of the key.

    A StoreKey is unique and cannot conflict with another key.
    """

    __slots__ = ()


class Store:
    """Store is a type-safe heterogenous mutable mapping that
    allows keys and value types to be defined separately from
    where it (the Store) is created.

    Usually you will be given an object which has a ``Store``:

    .. code-block:: python

        store: Store = some_object.store

    If a module wants to store data in this Store, it creates StoreKeys
    for its keys (at the module level):

    .. code-block:: python

        some_str_key = StoreKey[str]()
        some_bool_key = StoreKey[bool]()

    To store information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # Value type must match the key.
        store[some_str_key] = "value"
        store[some_bool_key] = True

    To retrieve the information:

    .. code-block:: python

        # The static type of some_str is str.
        some_str = store[some_str_key]
        # The static type of some_bool is bool.
        some_bool = store[some_bool_key]

    Why use this?
    -------------

    Problem: module Internal defines an object. Module External, which
    module Internal doesn't know about, receives the object and wants to
    attach information to it, to be retrieved later given the object.

    Bad solution 1: Module External assigns private attributes directly on
    the object. This doesn't work well because the type checker doesn't
    know about these attributes and it complains about undefined attributes.

    Bad solution 2: module Internal adds a ``Dict[str, Any]`` attribute to
    the object. Module External stores its data in private keys of this dict.
    This doesn't work well because retrieved values are untyped.

    Good solution: module Internal adds a ``Store`` to the object. Module
    External mints StoreKeys for its own keys. Module External stores and
    retrieves its data using these keys.
    """

    __slots__ = ("_store",)

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store = {}  # type: Dict[StoreKey[Any], object]

    def __setitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T], value: T) -> None:
        """Set a value for key."""
        self._store[key] = value

    def __getitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> T:
        """Get the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        return cast(T, self._store[key])

    def get(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: D) -> Union[T, D]:
        """Get the value for key, or return default if the key wasn't set
        before."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            return default

    def setdefault(self, key: StoreKey[T], default: T) -> T:
        """Return the value of key if already set, otherwise set the value
        of key to default and return default."""
        try:
            return self[key]
        except KeyError:
            self[key] = default
            return default

    def __delitem__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> None:
        """Delete the value for key.

        Raises KeyError if the key wasn't set before.
        """
        del self._store[key]

    def __contains__(self, key: StoreKey[T]) -> bool:
        """Returns whether key was set."""
        return key in self._store

def pytest_addoption(parser: Parser) -> None:
    group = parser.getgroup("general")
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures",
        "--funcargs",
        action="store_true",
        dest="showfixtures",
        default=False,
        help="show available fixtures, sorted by plugin appearance "
        "(fixtures with leading '_' are only shown with '-v')",
    )
    group.addoption(
        "--fixtures-per-test",
        action="store_true",
        dest="show_fixtures_per_test",
        default=False,
        help="show fixtures per test",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_files",
        type="args",
        # NOTE: default is also used in AssertionRewritingHook.
        default=["test_*.py", "*_test.py"],
        help="glob-style file patterns for Python test module discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_classes",
        type="args",
        default=["Test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test class discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "python_functions",
        type="args",
        default=["test"],
        help="prefixes or glob names for Python test function and method discovery",
    )
    parser.addini(
        "disable_test_id_escaping_and_forfeit_all_rights_to_community_support",
        type="bool",
        default=False,
        help="disable string escape non-ascii characters, might cause unwanted "
        "side effects(use at your own risk)",
    )


def pytest_cmdline_main(config: Config) -> Optional[Union[int, ExitCode]]:
    if config.option.showfixtures:
        showfixtures(config)
        return 0
    if config.option.show_fixtures_per_test:
        show_fixtures_per_test(config)
        return 0
    return None


def pytest_generate_tests(metafunc: "Metafunc") -> None:
    for marker in metafunc.definition.iter_markers(name="parametrize"):
        # TODO: Fix this type-ignore (overlapping kwargs).
        metafunc.parametrize(*marker.args, **marker.kwargs, _param_mark=marker)  # type: ignore[misc]


def pytest_configure(config: Config) -> None:
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "parametrize(argnames, argvalues): call a test function multiple "
        "times passing in different arguments in turn. argvalues generally "
        "needs to be a list of values if argnames specifies only one name "
        "or a list of tuples of values if argnames specifies multiple names. "
        "Example: @parametrize('arg1', [1,2]) would lead to two calls of the "
        "decorated test function, one with arg1=1 and another with arg1=2."
        "see https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/parametrize.html for more info "
        "and examples.",
    )
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "usefixtures(fixturename1, fixturename2, ...): mark tests as needing "
        "all of the specified fixtures. see "
        "https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/fixture.html#usefixtures ",
    )


def async_warn_and_skip(nodeid: str) -> None:
    msg = "async def functions are not natively supported and have been skipped.\n"
    msg += (
        "You need to install a suitable plugin for your async framework, for example:\n"
    )
    msg += "  - pytest-asyncio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-trio\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-tornasync\n"
    msg += "  - pytest-twisted"
    warnings.warn(PytestUnhandledCoroutineWarning(msg.format(nodeid)))
    skip(msg="async def function and no async plugin installed (see warnings)")


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pyfunc_call(pyfuncitem: "Function") -> Optional[object]:
    testfunction = pyfuncitem.obj
    if is_async_function(testfunction):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    funcargs = pyfuncitem.funcargs
    testargs = {arg: funcargs[arg] for arg in pyfuncitem._fixtureinfo.argnames}
    result = testfunction(**testargs)
    if hasattr(result, "__await__") or hasattr(result, "__aiter__"):
        async_warn_and_skip(pyfuncitem.nodeid)
    return True


def pytest_collect_file(path: py.path.local, parent) -> Optional["Module"]:
    ext = path.ext
    if ext == ".py":
        if not parent.session.isinitpath(path):
            if not path_matches_patterns(
                path, parent.config.getini("python_files") + ["__init__.py"]
            ):
                return None
        ihook = parent.session.gethookproxy(path)
        module = ihook.pytest_pycollect_makemodule(
            path=path, parent=parent
        )  # type: Module
        return module
    return None


def path_matches_patterns(path: py.path.local, patterns: Iterable[str]) -> bool:
    """Returns True if path matches any of the patterns in the list of globs given."""
    return any(path.fnmatch(pattern) for pattern in patterns)


def pytest_pycollect_makemodule(path: py.path.local, parent) -> "Module":
    if path.basename == "__init__.py":
        pkg = Package.from_parent(parent, fspath=path)  # type: Package
        return pkg
    mod = Module.from_parent(parent, fspath=path)  # type: Module
    return mod


@hookimpl(trylast=True)
def pytest_pycollect_makeitem(collector: "PyCollector", name: str, obj: object):
    # nothing was collected elsewhere, let's do it here
    if safe_isclass(obj):
        if collector.istestclass(obj, name):
            return Class.from_parent(collector, name=name, obj=obj)
    elif collector.istestfunction(obj, name):
        # mock seems to store unbound methods (issue473), normalize it
        obj = getattr(obj, "__func__", obj)
        # We need to try and unwrap the function if it's a functools.partial
        # or a functools.wrapped.
        # We mustn't if it's been wrapped with mock.patch (python 2 only)
        if not (inspect.isfunction(obj) or inspect.isfunction(get_real_func(obj))):
            filename, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
            warnings.warn_explicit(
                message=PytestCollectionWarning(
                    "cannot collect %r because it is not a function." % name
                ),
                category=None,
                filename=str(filename),
                lineno=lineno + 1,
            )
        elif getattr(obj, "__test__", True):
            if is_generator(obj):
                res = Function.from_parent(collector, name=name)
                reason = "yield tests were removed in pytest 4.0 - {name} will be ignored".format(
                    name=name
                )
                res.add_marker(MARK_GEN.xfail(run=False, reason=reason))
                res.warn(PytestCollectionWarning(reason))
            else:
                res = list(collector._genfunctions(name, obj))
            return res


class PyobjMixin:
    _ALLOW_MARKERS = True

    # Function and attributes that the mixin needs (for type-checking only).
    if TYPE_CHECKING:
        name = ""  # type: str
        parent = None  # type: Optional[nodes.Node]
        own_markers = []  # type: List[Mark]

        def getparent(self, cls: Type[nodes._NodeType]) -> Optional[nodes._NodeType]:
            ...

        def listchain(self) -> List[nodes.Node]:
            ...

    @property
    def module(self):
        """Python module object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Module)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def cls(self):
        """Python class object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Class)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def instance(self):
        """Python instance object this node was collected from (can be None)."""
        node = self.getparent(Instance)
        return node.obj if node is not None else None

    @property
    def obj(self):
        """Underlying Python object."""
        obj = getattr(self, "_obj", None)
        if obj is None:
            self._obj = obj = self._getobj()
            # XXX evil hack
            # used to avoid Instance collector marker duplication
            if self._ALLOW_MARKERS:
                self.own_markers.extend(get_unpacked_marks(self.obj))
        return obj

    @obj.setter
    def obj(self, value):
        self._obj = value

    def _getobj(self):
        """Gets the underlying Python object. May be overwritten by subclasses."""
        # TODO: Improve the type of `parent` such that assert/ignore aren't needed.
        assert self.parent is not None
        obj = self.parent.obj  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
        return getattr(obj, self.name)

    def getmodpath(self, stopatmodule: bool = True, includemodule: bool = False) -> str:
        """ return python path relative to the containing module. """
        chain = self.listchain()
        chain.reverse()
        parts = []
        for node in chain:
            if isinstance(node, Instance):
                continue
            name = node.name
            if isinstance(node, Module):
                name = os.path.splitext(name)[0]
                if stopatmodule:
                    if includemodule:
                        parts.append(name)
                    break
            parts.append(name)
        parts.reverse()
        return ".".join(parts)

    def reportinfo(self) -> Tuple[Union[py.path.local, str], int, str]:
        # XXX caching?
        obj = self.obj
        compat_co_firstlineno = getattr(obj, "compat_co_firstlineno", None)
        if isinstance(compat_co_firstlineno, int):
            # nose compatibility
            file_path = sys.modules[obj.__module__].__file__
            if file_path.endswith(".pyc"):
                file_path = file_path[:-1]
            fspath = file_path  # type: Union[py.path.local, str]
            lineno = compat_co_firstlineno
        else:
            fspath, lineno = getfslineno(obj)
        modpath = self.getmodpath()
        assert isinstance(lineno, int)
        return fspath, lineno, modpath


class PyCollector(PyobjMixin, nodes.Collector):
    def funcnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_functions", name)

    def isnosetest(self, obj: object) -> bool:
        """ Look for the __test__ attribute, which is applied by the
        @nose.tools.istest decorator
        """
        # We explicitly check for "is True" here to not mistakenly treat
        # classes with a custom __getattr__ returning something truthy (like a
        # function) as test classes.
        return safe_getattr(obj, "__test__", False) is True

    def classnamefilter(self, name: str) -> bool:
        return self._matches_prefix_or_glob_option("python_classes", name)

    def istestfunction(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        if self.funcnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj):
            if isinstance(obj, staticmethod):
                # static methods need to be unwrapped
                obj = safe_getattr(obj, "__func__", False)
            return (
                safe_getattr(obj, "__call__", False)
                and fixtures.getfixturemarker(obj) is None
            )
        else:
            return False

    def istestclass(self, obj: object, name: str) -> bool:
        return self.classnamefilter(name) or self.isnosetest(obj)

    def _matches_prefix_or_glob_option(self, option_name: str, name: str) -> bool:
        """
        checks if the given name matches the prefix or glob-pattern defined
        in ini configuration.
        """
        for option in self.config.getini(option_name):
            if name.startswith(option):
                return True
            # check that name looks like a glob-string before calling fnmatch
            # because this is called for every name in each collected module,
            # and fnmatch is somewhat expensive to call
            elif ("*" in option or "?" in option or "[" in option) and fnmatch.fnmatch(
                name, option
            ):
                return True
        return False

    def collect(self) -> Iterable[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]:
        if not getattr(self.obj, "__test__", True):
            return []

        # NB. we avoid random getattrs and peek in the __dict__ instead
        # (XXX originally introduced from a PyPy need, still true?)
        dicts = [getattr(self.obj, "__dict__", {})]
        for basecls in self.obj.__class__.__mro__:
            dicts.append(basecls.__dict__)
        seen = set()  # type: Set[str]
        values = []  # type: List[Union[nodes.Item, nodes.Collector]]

Thought: The add_marker implementation seems normal. The bug is that dynamically added xfail markers should cause the test failure to be treated as xfail. The code in skipping.evaluate_xfail_marks iterates over item.iter_markers(name="xfail"), which checks the node chain reversed list; iter_markers yields node, mark for reversed(listchain) and iterates node.own_markers. So dynamic markers added to own_markers should be seen.

Why did behavior change in pytest 6? There may be a change in when evaluate_xfail_marks is called: in pytest_runtest_setup it sets xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and stores it, but request.node.add_marker is called during test function runtime; in the user's test they call request.node.add_marker inside the test body, after setup, but before the assertion triggers. In pytest 5, evaluate_xfail_marks was also called in pytest_runtest_makereport or runtest_call wrapper, so dynamic addition during call would be re-evaluated. In current code, pytest_runtest_call checks item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and if None, re-evaluates. But xfailed_key was set in setup to an Xfail or None. Let's trace:

- In pytest_runtest_setup (setup stage), if not runxfail: item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item). So if no xfail initially, xfailed_key stored as None. In runtest_call (hookwrapper), it gets xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None); if xfailed is None: item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item). So it re-evaluates at call time if previously None. Great.

But dynamic addition occurs during test call, i.e., inside test function; add_marker is called while runtest_call hookwrapper is executing? Let's see runtest_call implementation: it's a hookwrapper, so plugin flow: runtest_call yields, letting test be executed between pre and post hook code. evaluate_xfail_marks is done before yield, so before test runs. But add_marker happens during test run - too late for re-evaluation before test body. However, pytest 5 probably re-evaluated xfail in makereport based on markers present at time of report generation; indeed, in pytest_runtest_makereport they check item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and if report not skipped and xfailed: they evaluate call.excinfo and set rep.outcome accordingly. But if xfailed wasn't stored earlier and was None, they won't detect dynamic addition. In pytest 5 maybe the logic was different: in makereport they re-evaluated evaluate_xfail_marks when xfailed is None. Let's open older commit? But we have this repo state; maybe in 5.x, runtest_makereport code had a branch that re-evaluated xfail marks if xfailed was None. Let's search for xfailed_key usage.

Problem: Pytest 6: Dynamically adding xfail marker in test no longer ignores failure

With pytest 5.x, we can dynamically add an xfail to a test `request` object using `request.node.add_marker(mark)` (see example below). In 5.x this treated the failing test like a a test marked statically with an `xfail`. With 6.0.0rc0 it raises.

    request.node.add_marker(mark)
    assert 0

What I learned from reading:
- Node.add_marker (nodes.Node.add_marker) appends a Mark (via MarkDecorator.mark) into self.own_markers and updates self.keywords. So dynamically added markers are visible via item.iter_markers (which checks listchain() and node.own_markers).
- evaluate_xfail_marks(item) iterates item.iter_markers(name="xfail") and returns an Xfail dataclass when an xfail applies; it does not consult any stored state, just reads markers on the item chain.
- skipping.py defines two StoreKeys: skipped_by_mark_key and xfailed_key. Code sets item._store[xfailed_key] in pytest_runtest_setup to the result of evaluate_xfail_marks(item) (unless runxfail option set).
- pytest_runtest_call hook (a hookwrapper) reads item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and, if None, calls evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and stores result. Then it yields (test executes). Note: this re-evaluation happens before the test function runs (i.e., before yield).
- pytest_runtest_makereport uses xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and then decides whether to change report.outcome/wasxfail based on xfailed and on call.excinfo. It does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks when xfailed is None.
- The test case in the report calls request.node.add_marker(...) inside the test body. That means:
  - setup stage had no xfail marker -> item._store[xfailed_key] set to None (or set to None explicitly by setup)
  - runtest_call re-evaluation occurs but before running test body. The user's add_marker occurs during the test body, after that re-evaluation.
  - makereport reads item._store[xfailed_key] but since item._store[xfailed_key] was set earlier to None (or not set), makereport sees None and does NOT re-evaluate, so it treats the failure as a normal failure instead of xfail.
- In pytest 5.x the same code path apparently resulted in marking the failure as xfail when add_marker was called in the test. That implies that in pytest 5.x makereport probably re-evaluated xfail marks at report time (or otherwise detected dynamic markers added during test body). In pytest 6.x makereport no longer re-evaluates when xfailed is None.

1d) Clear statement of what the bug is and who is primarily responsible
- Bug: Dynamically adding an xfail marker to the test node during the test body (via request.node.add_marker(...) or request.applymarker inside the test) no longer causes a failing assertion to be reported as xfail; instead it is reported as a normal failure in pytest 6.x. In other words, dynamically added xfail markers during test execution are not considered when computing the final TestReport.
- Primary responsible code: pytest_runtest_makereport in src/_pytest/skipping.py (specifically, it reads xfailed from item._store but does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None). The logic that sets item._store[xfailed_key] in setup / call is correct, but makereport should re-evaluate when a marker may have been added after those checks (i.e., during the call stage). So the lack of re-evaluation in pytest_runtest_makereport is the root cause.

Implementation: for node in reversed(self.listchain()): for mark in node.own_markers: yield node, mark if name matches. So markers on the function (own_markers) are yielded before markers on parent nodes because listchain() yields chain root->...->self and reversed yields self->...->root. Thus dynamically appended own_markers are visible in iter_markers immediately after add_marker occurs.

Checkpoint 4 — Propose a fix, then challenge it

Proposed fix (summary)
- Problem: pytest_runtest_makereport reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) but does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if xfailed is None. That means markers added during the test body (after runtest_call's pre-yield re-evaluation) are not considered when building the final TestReport.
- Fix: In pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py), if the stored xfailed is None, re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and update item._store[xfailed_key], so dynamic xfail markers added during the test body will be detected and the final report updated accordingly.

Exact change (patch snippet)

File: src/_pytest/skipping.py

Replace (current code around the start of pytest_runtest_makereport):

    @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
    def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
        outcome = yield
        rep = outcome.get_result()
        xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
        # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
        if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
            ...

with updated code that re-evaluates when xfailed is None:

    @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
    def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
        outcome = yield
        rep = outcome.get_result()
        xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
        # If xfailed was not computed earlier (None), re-evaluate now so that
        # markers added during the test call (e.g. request.node.add_marker)
        # are respected when building the report.
        if xfailed is None:
            item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
        # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
        if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
            ...

(One-line insertion: the if xfailed is None: ... evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and assign back to item._store.)

Rationale: This mirrors the pre-call logic in pytest_runtest_call (which re-evaluates before yield if None), but ensures evaluation also happens as late as possible (in makereport) so dynamically added markers during the call are picked up for final report generation.

Now the required challenge questions:

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence: The current code assumes that the xfail evaluation performed in setup or just before the call (runtest_call) will always be final and need not be re-evaluated during report generation; my fix removes that assumption by allowing xfail to be evaluated at makereport time as a last chance to detect markers added during the call.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- I inspected the component classes (nodes.Node, mark.structures.Mark, and evaluate_xfail_marks). There are no numeric hardcoded values that encode the timing assumption. The "assumption" was encoded not via a literal constant but via control-flow: pytest_runtest_call re-evaluates before yield, and pytest_runtest_makereport did not re-evaluate. The relevant "value" is item._store[xfailed_key] which gets set in pytest_runtest_setup or possibly earlier; if that store contains None it previously was only re-evaluated in pytest_runtest_call (pre-yield) — not at makereport. No numeric constants need changing.

- I verified the only related hardcoded/kind-of-constant behavior is evaluate_xfail_marks default parameters (run default True, strict uses ini). Those are unaffected.

Conclusion: No hardcoded numeric values must become dynamic — we only change control flow to re-evaluate at makereport time.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- We changed report generation to re-evaluate xfail marks; the complementary operations are:
  - pytest_runtest_setup: sets item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) before the test starts (unless --runxfail). That remains correct.
  - pytest_runtest_call: before calling test, it re-evaluates if xfailed is None and may call xfail(...) to skip run=False cases. That remains correct.
  - pytest_runtest_makereport: previously only read item._store and used that value (possibly None) to decide report outcome; we now re-evaluate if xfailed is None. The reverse operation is: if marking happens earlier (setup or fixture) we may have stored a non-None xfailed prior to call; in that case makereport will not re-evaluate because stored xfailed is not None — that is fine because it's already known.
- I re-read pytest_runtest_makereport's surrounding logic (skipping.py) to ensure no code path expects xfailed to be absent: makereport already handles call.excinfo being xfail.Exception and the unexpectedsuccess unittest path. Re-evaluation at makereport time simply enables detection of markers added during test call, with no interference with other logic like runxfail option (which makereport already skips).
- Concretely, trace of variables for the parent's complementary methods (runner.call_and_report -> makereport):
  - call_and_report calls call_runtest_hook -> runtest_call hookwrapper -> runtest_call (which re-evaluates pre-yield only if xfailed is None), then yield, test runs (dynamic add_marker may happen here), then runtest_call completes.
  - call_and_report then calls hook.pytest_runtest_makereport(item=item, call=call). Our change ensures that if runtime add_marker occurred, makereport will recompute xfailed and then act accordingly.
- No parent's method needs to be overridden because the runner's logic is still valid. We do need makereport to re-evaluate (we add that). The existing pre-call re-evaluation remains as-is (no conflict).

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- Methods touched: only pytest_runtest_makereport (we add a small re-evaluation). All other methods (pytest_runtest_setup, pytest_runtest_call, Node.add_marker, evaluate_xfail_marks, iter_markers, MarkDecorator) still work as before.
- Concrete verification:
  - Node.add_marker continues to append to own_markers and update keywords.
  - evaluate_xfail_marks still reads item.iter_markers (which will reflect own_markers appended during test body).
  - pytest_runtest_call still re-evaluates at pre-yield if store has None (unchanged).
  - pytest_runtest_makereport now re-evaluates if store has None; this only widens detection window. If store previously had a non-None xfailed (set during setup or pre-call), we do not re-evaluate (we could but no need).
- Edge cases: If a plugin previously expected xfailed to be computed only before the call and not later, this change still only reads markers; it does not change any other fields in item._store beyond setting xfailed_key to the evaluated result (same as other places do). This is consistent with the existing design where pre-call or setup code sets that store key. So all methods continue to work.

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods are necessary. The parent runner's hook invocation remains the same. We only add a re-evaluation line in pytest_runtest_makereport. There is no need to override any other parent's method.
- I checked the parent's method list (runner.call_and_report and hooks) and read the actual implementations to ensure this is sufficient — yes.

Checkpoint 5 — Final report (what to change and why, full fix)

Root cause
- pytest did not re-evaluate xfail markers at the time the final TestReport is made; xfail evaluation was only done in setup and pre-call (runtest_call pre-yield). If a test adds an xfail marker dynamically during its execution (i.e., after the pre-call re-evaluation), that marker was ignored by pytest_runtest_makereport and the test failure was reported as a normal failure. Pytest 5.x apparently re-evaluated at report time (or otherwise handled this case), but pytest 6.x's skipping.py no longer re-evaluated in makereport — hence the regression.

Every location that needs change
1) Primary change: src/_pytest/skipping.py:pytest_runtest_makereport
   - Current behavior: xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) and no re-evaluation if None.
   - Problem: dynamic markers added during test call are not considered.
   - Fix: If xfailed is None, call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and store the result back into item._store[xfailed_key] so makereport uses the up-to-date markers.

   Exact code change (context + patch):

   Before (excerpt):

       @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
       def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
           outcome = yield
           rep = outcome.get_result()
           xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
           # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
           if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
               ...

   After (excerpt):

       @hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
       def pytest_runtest_makereport(item: Item, call: CallInfo[None]):
           outcome = yield
           rep = outcome.get_result()
           xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None)
           # If xfailed wasn't previously evaluated (None), re-evaluate here
           # so that markers added during the call stage (e.g. via
           # request.node.add_marker) are taken into account when creating the
           # final report.
           if xfailed is None:
               item._store[xfailed_key] = xfailed = evaluate_xfail_marks(item)
           # unittest special case, see setting of unexpectedsuccess_key
           if unexpectedsuccess_key in item._store and rep.when == "call":
               ...

Why this is complete (addresses every affected location)
- The only place that failed to reflect dynamically added markers at the time the report is constructed was pytest_runtest_makereport. Setup and runtest_call already re-evaluate earlier. By adding a re-evaluation in makereport we ensure:
  - Markers dynamically added during setup / fixture setup (before runtest_call yield) continue to work (they were already handled by setup/runtest_call).
  - Markers dynamically added during the test body (after runtest_call pre-yield) will now be seen at makereport time and will influence rep.wasxfail / rep.outcome, thus restoring the pytest 5.x behavior for the user case in the bug report.

Any hardcoded values that need to become dynamic
- None. There were no numeric constants encoding timing assumptions. The change is a control-flow change (re-evaluation at makereport), not replacing any hardcoded numbers.

Any methods that need to be added/overridden
- None. All changes are local to pytest_runtest_makereport in src/_pytest/skipping.py.

Testing notes (how this fixes the user's reproduction)
- Original minimal repro (from the report):

    def test_xfail_test(request):
        mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
        request.node.add_marker(mark)
        assert 0

  - With the proposed change, evaluate_xfail_marks will return an Xfail when makereport runs (because item.iter_markers(name="xfail") will see the marker added to own_markers), so the final report will mark the assertion failure as xfail rather than a normal failure.

Why this is safe
- The change mirrors the same evaluation used earlier in setup and runtest_call; it assigns the result into item._store[xfailed_key] (consistent with other places that store it).
- It only executes if item._store[xfailed_key] is None — i.e., we don't overwrite an earlier computed xfailed from setup or pre-call.
- The evaluate_xfail_marks uses the same public API for reading markers and deciding run/strict/raises, so no new invariants are introduced.

Follow-up / Optional considerations
- If desired, one could log or document that xfail can be determined as late as report-time if the markers are added during the call. But functional behavior is what users expect (pytest 5.x behavior). No behavioral change for code paths where xfail is set earlier.
- I checked tests in testing/test_skipping.py for dynamic xfail usage: the existing tests cover dynamic xfail added during fixture setup. This change extends correctness to markers added in the test body as well.

Summary
- Root cause: pytest_runtest_makereport didn't re-evaluate xfail when item._store[xfailed_key] was None, so markers added during the test body were ignored for the final report.
- Fix: In pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py), if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) and store the result before computing the report outcome.

- This confirms that when request.node.add_marker(...) is called during test execution, the marker is visible through item.iter_markers(name="xfail") at any later time in the test lifecycle (including at makereport) — provided the caller re-evaluates markers at that time.

- There are tests for adding xfail during fixture setup and similar pre-call phases, which rely on the pre-call re-evaluation in pytest_runtest_call or setup. bug_report.Title: Regression: dynamically-added xfail (during test body) is ignored in pytest 6.x; failure reported as normal failure
bug_report.Description: If a test adds an xfail marker to its own node during test execution (for example, calling request.node.add_marker(pytest.mark.xfail(...)) inside the test body), pytest 6.x reports a failing assertion as a normal failure instead of an xfailed test. In pytest 5.x the same pattern resulted in an XFAIL outcome.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) pytest_runtest_makereport (src/_pytest/skipping.py) reads xfailed = item._store.get(xfailed_key, None) but does not call evaluate_xfail_marks(item) when the stored value is None. Because evaluation is not performed at report time, xfail markers added after the pre-call evaluation (i.e., markers appended during the test body via request.node.add_marker or request.applymarker) are not observed and therefore do not influence the final TestReport.

2) The control-flow/assumption encoded across hooks is as follows: pytest_runtest_setup sets item._store[xfailed_key] = evaluate_xfail_marks(item) during setup; pytest_runtest_call re-evaluates evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, but this re-evaluation happens before the test body runs (it is done pre-yield in the hookwrapper). pytest_runtest_makereport lacked a final evaluation of evaluate_xfail_marks(item) as a last chance to detect markers added during the call, so dynamically added 'xfail' markers in the test body are ignored when building the report. This missing final evaluation is the regression.

Locations encoding the incorrect assumption (each given equal weight):
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_makereport — reads item._store[xfailed_key] but does not re-evaluate evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if the stored value is None, so markers added during the call are not taken into account at report construction.
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_setup — evaluates xfail during setup and stores it in item._store[xfailed_key] (this is correct for markers added before test body execution).
- src/_pytest/skipping.py::pytest_runtest_call — re-evaluates evaluate_xfail_marks(item) if item._store[xfailed_key] is None, but that re-evaluation occurs before the test body runs; it therefore cannot detect markers added during the test body.

3) The relationship that must hold: when creating the final TestReport, xfailed must reflect evaluate_xfail_marks(item) executed at report time if the stored value is None, because item.iter_markers(name="xfail") will include markers appended to item.own_markers during the test body. The stored item._store[xfailed_key] should therefore be computed or re-checked at makereport time in cases where it is not already set to a non-None value.
StepsToReproduce: Create test_foo.py with contents:
import pytest
def test_xfail_test(request):
mark = pytest.mark.xfail(reason="xfail")
request.node.add_marker(mark)
assert 0
Run pytest on the file with pytest 6.x (for example: pytest -rsx test_foo.py).
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: If an xfail marker is added to the test node during test execution (inside the test body), a subsequent failure in the same test should be reported as XFAIL in the final test report (i.e., treated like a test marked xfail).
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: With pytest 6.x, when a test adds an xfail marker during the test body and then fails, the failure is reported as a normal failure (F) rather than XFAIL. In pytest 5.x the same test was reported as XFAIL.
