bug_report.Title: Confusing/incorrect guidance when pytest.skip() is used at module import-time (missing mention of allow_module_level or skip_module)
bug_report.Description: When pytest.skip(...) is called during module import (module-level), pytest raises a CollectError with a message that discourages module-level skipping and points to pytestmark, but the message does not mention the existing allow_module_level option (skip(..., allow_module_level=True)) nor the existence of other module-level skip paths. This makes correct usage unclear: the code already supports module-level skipping when allow_module_level=True (and importorskip uses that), but the message suggests module-level skip is not allowed.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) Misleading error message location and content (src/_pytest/python.py, Module._importtestmodule): Module._importtestmodule catches skip.Exception (the Skipped outcome) and, when e.allow_module_level is False, raises a CollectError whose message begins "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." and points users to pytestmark. That message omits any mention of the allow_module_level flag on the Skipped exception or of any convenience helper; as a result it discourages a supported escape hatch.

2) The outcomes API already defines an explicit escape hatch for module-level skipping (src/_pytest/outcomes.py): the Skipped exception and the skip() function accept an allow_module_level boolean parameter (default allow_module_level=False). The default encodes a conservative assumption (module-level skip is disallowed unless explicitly opted-in), but the error message in Module._importtestmodule does not surface this existing option. (See Skipped.__init__(..., allow_module_level: bool = False) and skip(msg, *, allow_module_level: bool = False).)

3) Another existing API mirrors module-level skip semantics (src/_pytest/outcomes.py, importorskip): importorskip raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) to indicate module-level skip on failed import. This shows that module-level skipping is intentionally supported in at least one public helper, yet the CollectError message discouraging pytest.skip at module-level does not make that relationship clear.

4) There is no ergonomics-focused convenience helper named skip_module in the codebase (search: no skip_module symbol defined). The combination of the discouraging message and absence of a clearly-named helper reduces discoverability of the allow_module_level escape-hatch.
StepsToReproduce: Create a module using Python 3.8-only syntax (e.g., `def foo(a, /, b): return a + b`) and a test file that imports that module.
Run pytest with an older Python interpreter (e.g., 3.6 or 3.7) and attempt to skip the module by calling pytest.skip("Requires Python >= 3.8") at module top-level (no allow_module_level).

Observe that pytest reports a CollectError and prints a message starting: "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." suggesting pytestmark usage but not mentioning `allow_module_level=True` nor any convenience helper.

Contrast that with calling pytest.skip("Requires Python >= 3.8", allow_module_level=True) during module import: that results in a module-level skip and no CollectError. Also note pytest.importorskip(...) raises Skipped(..., allow_module_level=True) when import fails, demonstrating the code supports module-level skipping.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: User-visible guidance should accurately reflect the library's supported API: when pytest.skip is invoked at module import-time the guidance should explain that module-level skipping is possible via pytest.skip(<reason>, allow_module_level=True) (and that importorskip already uses allow_module_level=True), while still recommending marks for skipping individual tests when appropriate. The message should not imply module-level skip is categorically unsupported when the code provides an explicit opt-in flag.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: pytest raises a CollectError with the message that begins "Using pytest.skip outside of a test is not allowed..." and directs users to use pytest.mark or pytestmark for module skipping, without mentioning the existing allow_module_level parameter on pytest.skip or any convenience helper. This leads users to believe module-level skipping is unsupported even though code paths like importorskip use allow_module_level=True to skip modules.

method_cache.src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py:
"""Find all assert statements in *mod* and rewrite them."""
        if not mod.body:
            # Nothing to do.
            return

        # We'll insert some special imports at the top of the module, but after any
        # docstrings and __future__ imports, so first figure out where that is.
        doc = getattr(mod, "docstring", None)
        expect_docstring = doc is None
        if doc is not None and self.is_rewrite_disabled(doc):
            return
        pos = 0
        lineno = 1
        for item in mod.body:
            if (
                expect_docstring
                and isinstance(item, ast.Expr)
                and isinstance(item.value, ast.Str)
            ):
                doc = item.value.s
                if self.is_rewrite_disabled(doc):
                    return
                expect_docstring = False
            elif (
                isinstance(item, ast.ImportFrom)
                and item.level == 0
                and item.module == "__future__"
            ):
                pass
            else:
                break
            pos += 1
        # Special case: for a decorated function, set the lineno to that of the
        # first decorator, not the `def`. Issue #4984.
        if isinstance(item, ast.FunctionDef) and item.decorator_list:
            lineno = item.decorator_list[0].lineno
        else:
            lineno = item.lineno
        # Now actually insert the special imports.
        if sys.version_info >= (3, 10):
            aliases = [
                ast.alias("builtins", "@py_builtins", lineno=lineno, col_offset=0),
                ast.alias(
                    "_pytest.assertion.rewrite",
                    "@pytest_ar",
                    lineno=lineno,
                    col_offset=0,
                ),
            ]
        else:
            aliases = [
                ast.alias("builtins", "@py_builtins"),
                ast.alias("_pytest.assertion.rewrite", "@pytest_ar"),
            ]
        imports = [
            ast.Import([alias], lineno=lineno, col_offset=0) for alias in aliases
        ]
        mod.body[pos:pos] = imports

        # Collect asserts.
        nodes: List[ast.AST] = [mod]

# disable flake check on this file because some constructs are strange

import ast
import inspect
import linecache
import sys
import textwrap
from pathlib import Path
from types import CodeType
from typing import Any
from typing import Dict
from typing import Optional

import pytest
from _pytest._code import Code
from _pytest._code import Frame
from _pytest._code import getfslineno
from _pytest._code import Source
from _pytest.pathlib import import_path


def test_source_str_function() -> None:
    x = Source("3")
    assert str(x) == "3"

    x = Source("   3")
    assert str(x) == "3"

    x = Source(
        """
        3
        """
    )
    assert str(x) == "\n3"


def test_source_from_function() -> None:
    source = Source(test_source_str_function)
    assert str(source).startswith("def test_source_str_function() -> None:")


def test_source_from_method() -> None:
    class TestClass:
        def test_method(self):
            pass

    source = Source(TestClass().test_method)
    assert source.lines == ["def test_method(self):", "    pass"]


def test_source_from_lines() -> None:
    lines = ["a \n", "b\n", "c"]
    source = Source(lines)
    assert source.lines == ["a ", "b", "c"]


def test_source_from_inner_function() -> None:
    def f():
        raise NotImplementedError()

    source = Source(f)
    assert str(source).startswith("def f():")


def test_source_strips() -> None:
    source = Source("")
    assert source == Source()
    assert str(source) == ""
    assert source.strip() == source


def test_source_strip_multiline() -> None:
    source = Source()

def test_findsource(monkeypatch) -> None:
    from _pytest._code.source import findsource

    filename = "<pytest-test_findsource>"
    lines = ["if 1:\n", "    def x():\n", "          pass\n"]
    co = compile("".join(lines), filename, "exec")

    # Type ignored because linecache.cache is private.
    monkeypatch.setitem(linecache.cache, filename, (1, None, lines, filename))  # type: ignore[attr-defined]

    src, lineno = findsource(co)
    assert src is not None
    assert "if 1:" in str(src)

    d: Dict[str, Any] = {}
    eval(co, d)
    src, lineno = findsource(d["x"])
    assert src is not None
    assert "if 1:" in str(src)
    assert src[lineno] == "    def x():"

def getstatement(lineno: int, source) -> Source:

def test_comments() -> None:
    source = '''def test():
    "comment 1"
    x = 1
      # comment 2
    # comment 3

    assert False

"""
comment 4
"""
'''
    for line in range(2, 6):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == "    x = 1"
    if sys.version_info >= (3, 8) or hasattr(sys, "pypy_version_info"):
        tqs_start = 8
    else:
        tqs_start = 10
        assert str(getstatement(10, source)) == '"""'
    for line in range(6, tqs_start):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == "    assert False"
    for line in range(tqs_start, 10):
        assert str(getstatement(line, source)) == '"""\ncomment 4\n"""'

def test_source_with_decorator() -> None:
    """Test behavior with Source / Code().source with regard to decorators."""
    from _pytest.compat import get_real_func

    @pytest.mark.foo
    def deco_mark():
        assert False

    src = inspect.getsource(deco_mark)
    assert textwrap.indent(str(Source(deco_mark)), "    ") + "\n" == src
    assert src.startswith("    @pytest.mark.foo")

    @pytest.fixture
    def deco_fixture():
        assert False

    src = inspect.getsource(deco_fixture)
    assert src == "    @pytest.fixture\n    def deco_fixture():\n        assert False\n"
    # currenly Source does not unwrap decorators, testing the
    # existing behavior here for explicitness, but perhaps we should revisit/change this
    # in the future
    assert str(Source(deco_fixture)).startswith("@functools.wraps(function)")
    assert (
        textwrap.indent(str(Source(get_real_func(deco_fixture))), "    ") + "\n" == src
    )

Checkpoint 3: Map the architecture

3a) Inspect component functions (get_statement_startend2 and getstatementrange_ast)
- I already read these fully. Notes on constants and assumptions:

  - AST line numbers: get_statement_startend2 uses x.lineno - 1 for ast.stmt nodes. That encodes the assumption that x.lineno points to the first line of the statement in the source (AST lineno attributes are 1-based; subtract 1 to get 0-based line index).
  - For 'finalbody' and 'orelse', the code appends val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 (i.e. subtracts 2). This is a hardcoded offset whose rationale is not obvious at a glance (a comment says "Treat the finally/orelse part as its own statement.") — that -1 -1 encodes an assumption about how AST linenos for orelse/finally are positioned relative to boundaries. This is a suspicious hardcoded adjustment.
  - BlockFinder usage: getstatementrange_ast uses inspect.BlockFinder/tokenize to ensure the found start..end doesn't span differently-indented blocks. It uses block_finder.started = source.lines[start][0].isspace() to set started mode. Then it tokenizes the slice source.lines[start:end] and passes each token to block_finder.tokeneater; if inspect.EndOfBlock or IndentationError occurs, it sets end = block_finder.last + start. This logic encodes an assumption about tokenization and block_finder.last being a 0-based count of lines that belong to the block starting at 'start'. It is sensitive to off-by-one issues if node.lineno semantics change.
  - Later trimming of trailing blank/comments (lines 206–211) trims # comments and blank lines at the end of the range, but does not trim decorator lines.

Hardcoded numbers / constants found:
- '- 1' uses to convert AST lineno from 1-based to 0-based.
- Additional '- 1 - 1' (i.e. -2) for finalbody/orelse starts — this is a hardcoded offset that could break if AST lineno semantics change.
- The code also checks start+1 for readability of end > start + 1 and uses block_finder.last + start.

Checkpoint 4: Propose a fix, then immediately challenge it

Proposed fix (short description)
- Root cause: On Python 3.9, AST lineno metadata for some nodes (notably FunctionDef/ClassDef with decorators) and how block boundaries are computed cause getstatementrange_ast to include the decorator line of a following function when computing the minimal statement region for a given lineno. Practically, the returned end index can include the decorator line which ends up being printed between the failing assertion line and the diff. The current code trims comments and blank lines at the end of the computed range but does not trim trailing decorator lines (lines beginning with '@') which are always associated with the following def/class statement and should not be included in the previous statement's source region.

- Minimal & robust fix: After the existing trimming of blank/comment lines in getstatementrange_ast, also trim trailing decorator lines (lines whose stripped text startswith('@')) from the computed end. This removes the '@t' line from the displayed assertion region.

Patch (concrete change)
- Modify function getstatementrange_ast in src/_pytest/_code/source.py adding an extra trimming loop just after the existing comment/empty-line trimming and before return:

  Insert something like:

      # Also trim trailing decorator lines (they belong to the next statement).
      while end:
          line = source.lines[end - 1].lstrip()
          if line.startswith("@"):
              end -= 1
          else:
              break

- This is intentionally conservative: it only removes trailing decorator lines (which always belong to the next def/class) from the statement region. It preserves existing logic for comments/blank lines and the block-finder adjustments. It does not change get_statement_startend2 numeric logic or the finalbody/orelse -2 adjustment (which could be potentially suspicious but covered by existing tests).

Now answer the required challenge questions.

4a) "What assumption does my fix change?"
- One-sentence: My fix removes the implicit assumption that the computed statement-range's trailing non-comment, non-empty lines may safely include decorator lines from the next statement — it enforces that trailing decorator lines do not belong to the previous statement's source region.

4b) "What hardcoded values encode this assumption?"
- Hardcoded constants noted in earlier inspection:
  - The '-1' subtraction (x.lineno - 1) to convert 1-based AST lineno to 0-based lines — still correct and unchanged.
  - The '- 1 - 1' (val[0].lineno - 1 - 1) used for 'finalbody' / 'orelse' — this remains unchanged in my fix. I considered whether this encodes problematic assumptions; it does, but it is unrelated to the decorator issue and is covered by the existing test-suite in this repository. Removing or changing it risks breaking other cases (try/except/else handling) unless fully re-evaluated. My fix does not alter these values directly.
- Are these still correct once the decorator assumption is changed?
  - The -1 conversions are still correct (they convert AST lineno 1-based to indices).
  - The mysterious -2 for orelse/finalbody still needs to be as-is for current test expectations; it is not directly implicated by the decorator trimming fix. Because my change only trims trailing decorator lines after other adjustments are done, those numeric offsets remain correct for other constructs.

4c) "What about the reverse operation?"
- By "reverse operation" we must check complementary operations that read/write or compute the same state. In this case, getstatementrange_ast is used to compute a slice of source lines for display — we must ensure any other logic that relies on the exact start/end values is still correct (for example, code that stores ast nodes, or formats the traceback).
- Relevant parent/consumer methods (from Checkpoint 3d):
  - TracebackEntry.getsource (calls getstatementrange_ast and returns source[start:end]).
  - Frame.statement / TracebackEntry.statement: these call Source.getstatement which invokes getstatementrange_ast (and return source[start:end]).
- I re-read TracebackEntry.getsource (code lines 243–269 in src/_pytest/_code/code.py).
  - It calls getstatementrange_ast(self.lineno, source, astnode=astnode), and then returns source[start:end], exactly as before.
- Concrete substitution / variable trace for one typical case (the bug case):
  - Variables:
    - source: Source object for whole file (lines list)
    - lineno: failing frame's 0-based line index of the assertion line.
  - Old getstatementrange_ast would produce (start, end) such that end might fall on or after the decorator line of the following function (so source[start:end] would include the decorator line if end points to a line after the decorator or if block_finder adjustments extended end).
  - New getstatementrange_ast will now further trim trailing decorator lines off end, so returned source[start:end] excludes decorator lines and shows only the assertion statement block as desired.
- Is any complementary method that writes data affected? No: we only change how the source range is selected for display; we are not changing AST rewriting or modifications to code. Nothing writes the selection back or relies on the old inclusion of decorator lines.
- Do any inherited or generic methods need to be overridden? There is no parent class with methods to override; consumers only call this function and will now get a slightly shorter slice (without trailing decorator lines). No additional overrides required.

4d) "Does every existing method still work?"
- The only modified function is getstatementrange_ast. Let's run through methods that use it:
  - Source.getstatement -> simply calls getstatementrange which calls getstatementrange_ast and returns source[start:end] => still works; it will produce same slices except with trailing decorator lines trimmed.
  - TracebackEntry.getsource / Frame.statement -> same effect as above.
  - Existing tests in testing/code/test_source.py cover many statement-range scenarios including decorated functions (test_source_with_decorator). My change trims trailing decorator lines only when computing a statement range that ends with decorator line(s). For tests that explicitly request the Source for the whole function (e.g., Source(deco_fixture)), they use inspect.getsource or Source constructed from function object rather than getstatementrange_ast — unaffected. test_source_with_decorator asserts that Source(get_real_func(deco_fixture)) equals the actual function source including the decorator; that's not affected since Source(function) uses inspect.getsource(rawcode) (see Source.__init__, lines 33–38) and not getstatementrange_ast.
- So existing behaviors are preserved for typical cases: we only affect the "getstatementrange for frame lineno" case, not the extraction of a function's full source (which still includes decorators).

4e) "Does any NEW method need to be added?"
- No new methods required. We add only a small trailing-decorator trimming loop inside getstatementrange_ast. No other functions need to be overridden or added because:
  - All consumers get a correct trimmed slice.
  - The complementary operations that read full function source use Source(function) / inspect.getsource and are not dependent on getstatementrange_ast semantics.

Checkpoint 5: Final report — root cause and complete fix

Root cause (summary)
- On newer Python versions (3.8/3.9+), AST lineno semantics around decorated functions (FunctionDef/ClassDef with decorator_list) and interactions with BlockFinder/tokenization can make the computed statement range include the decorator line(s) of the following function. Pytest's getstatementrange_ast trimmed trailing comments/blank lines but not decorator lines, so the decorator line of the next function was printed as part of the previous statement's display. This caused the extra '@t' line to appear in the assertion failure output on Python 3.9.

All locations to change
- Primary location: src/_pytest/_code/source.py — function getstatementrange_ast (single small change).
- No other files need changes. I reviewed rewrite.py (it already contains a special-case for setting lineno to decorator_list[0].lineno when inserting imports during assertion rewrite), so no further action is required in assertion.rewrite.

What each location currently does, what assumption is encoded, and what to change

1) src/_pytest/_code/source.py — getstatementrange_ast
   - Current behavior: Compute start,end for the minimal statement region containing the given lineno using get_statement_startend2, block-finder / tokenize adjustments, and trimming trailing blank/comment lines. Returns source[start:end].
   - Assumptions: That the AST-derived end value and block-finder adjustments will never produce trailing decorator lines; existing trimming removes blank/comment lines but not decorators.
   - Problem: Due to AST lineno semantics for decorated statements and block-finder interactions on newer CPython versions, end can end up pointing at (or beyond) the decorator line of the following function. The code does not trim decorator lines from the slice, so they get printed.
   - Fix: After the existing blank/comment trimming loop in getstatementrange_ast, add an additional loop to remove trailing lines that are decorators (source.lines[end-1].lstrip().startswith("@")) by decrementing end until the trailing line is not a decorator.

Why this fix is correct and complete
- Decorator lines always syntactically belong to the following def/class statement, never to the previous statement. Removing trailing '@' lines from the computed show-range is safe and yields what users expect: the assertion statement block only, not lines from the next function's decorator.
- The fix is narrow and conservative: it only trims trailing decorator lines, preserving the rest of the logic (block-finder adjustments, comment trimming) and therefore should not break other expected behaviors tested in the suite (the Source(...) of a function still uses inspect.getsource and will include decorators).
- All consumers of getstatementrange_ast (TracebackEntry.getsource, Frame.statement, etc.) will automatically receive the corrected slice; no other code changes are required.

Proposed patch (pseudo-diff)
- Insert this block into src/_pytest/_code/source.py inside getstatementrange_ast after the existing comment/blank trimming while loop (i.e., after line 211 in the file I inspected):

    # The end might still point to a comment or empty line, correct it.
    while end:
        line = source.lines[end - 1].lstrip()
        if line.startswith("#") or not line:
            end -= 1
        else:
            break

    # Also trim trailing decorator lines (they belong to the next statement).
    while end:
        line = source.lines[end - 1].lstrip()
        if line.startswith("@"):
            end -= 1
        else:
            break

- This adds only the decorator-trimming loop; the existing comment trimming loop is left intact to preserve tests that expect trailing comments removed.

Why I didn't change get_statement_startend2
- get_statement_startend2 contains a number of version-sensitive adjustments (the -1 and the -2 for orelse/finally). Altering those would be riskier and would require re-running the entire test-suite for a variety of Python versions, since they affect many statement-range behaviors (many are covered by tests in testing/code/test_source.py). The decorator trimming approach is simpler, robust, minimal, and fixes the symptom without touching other delicate numeric offsets.

Extra notes / justification
- rewrite.py already contains code that specially handles decorator_list when inserting imports (lines 712–716 in rewrite.py), which shows pytest is already aware of decorator-line semantic differences and that special handling may be needed elsewhere too. The trimming approach here is robust across different CPython versions (it doesn't rely on a particular AST lineno semantics; it just ensures that decorators are not included in previous statement slices).

Suggested tests to add (small unit tests)
- Add a test case similar to the user's example in testing/code/test_source.py to ensure getstatementrange (or TracebackEntry.statement) for the assertion line does not include subsequent decorator lines. Example:

    def test_assertion_followed_by_decorator_trim():
        src = Source(
            "def test_case():\n"
            "    assert 1 == 2\n"
            "\n"
            "    @dec\n"
            "    def inner():\n"
            "        pass\n"
        )
        # choose the assertion's 0-based line
        astnode, start, end = getstatementrange_ast(1, src)
        assert not any(line.lstrip().startswith("@") for line in src[start:end])

- This confirms the decorator-trimming behavior and prevents regression.

If you want, I can provide the exact patch (git diff / full file contents) for src/_pytest/_code/source.py with the decorator-trimming loop inserted at the correct location.

Thought: Phase 1 — Verification and completeness check

I will verify each claim in the draft report by inspecting the repository source and tests. Below I list what I checked, why, what I expected to learn, and what I found.

1) Why inspect src/_pytest/_code/source.py?
- Reasoning: The draft report names getstatementrange_ast and get_statement_startend2 (both in src/_pytest/_code/source.py) as the primary places that compute which source lines are shown for a failing assertion. I expected to confirm whether these functions:
  - compute start/end from AST lineno values using hardcoded offsets, and
  - trim trailing comment/blank lines but not decorator lines (which would explain the extra '@t' printed).
- Tool used: opened src/_pytest/_code/source.py (full file).

What I found (relevant excerpts):
- get_statement_startend2 collects statement line numbers from the AST and converts them to 0-based indices using x.lineno - 1; it also appends val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 for 'finalbody'/'orelse':

  From src/_pytest/_code/source.py:
  - lines 150–158:
    "for x in ast.walk(node):
         if isinstance(x, (ast.stmt, ast.ExceptHandler)):
             values.append(x.lineno - 1)
             for name in ('finalbody', 'orelse'):
                 val = getattr(x, name, None)
                 if val:
                     # Treat the finally/orelse part as its own statement.
                     values.append(val[0].lineno - 1 - 1)"

  This confirms the presence of the hardcoded offsets (x.lineno - 1 and val[0].lineno - 1 - 1) referenced in the draft.

- getstatementrange_ast calls get_statement_startend2, may adjust end using inspect.BlockFinder/tokenize, and then trims trailing comments/empty lines — but there is no trimming of decorator lines:

  From src/_pytest/_code/source.py:
  - lines 182–212:
    "start, end = get_statement_startend2(lineno, astnode)
     ...
     if end > start + 1:
         block_finder = inspect.BlockFinder()
         block_finder.started = source.lines[start][0].isspace()
         it = ((x + "\n") for x in source.lines[start:end])
         try:
             for tok in tokenize.generate_tokens(lambda: next(it)):
                 block_finder.tokeneater(*tok)
         except (inspect.EndOfBlock, IndentationError):
             end = block_finder.last + start
         except Exception:
             pass

     # The end might still point to a comment or empty line, correct it.
     while end:
         line = source.lines[end - 1].lstrip()
         if line.startswith("#") or not line:
             end -= 1
         else:
             break
     return astnode, start, end"

  This confirms that trailing blank/comment lines are trimmed but decorator lines (lines starting with '@') are not explicitly trimmed.

Conclusion from this file inspection:
- The draft claim that getstatementrange_ast can end up returning a range that includes decorator lines (because it does not trim them) is supported: decorator trimming is absent; the code trims only comments and blanks.
- The draft claim that get_statement_startend2 uses hardcoded offsets (x.lineno - 1 and val[0].lineno - 1 - 1) is supported.

2) Search for other occurrences and related code
- Reasoning: The investigation analysis mentioned other places where decorator/lineno semantics are handled; I wanted to check for similar code patterns or special-cases elsewhere in the codebase that indicate pytest was already aware of AST decorator-line semantics.
- Tool used: searched for decorator_list occurrences.

What I found:
- src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py contains a special-case when inserting imports at the top of a module: for a decorated function it sets lineno = item.decorator_list[0].lineno (lines 712–716). This shows that another part of pytest already had to handle decorator-line semantics explicitly:

  From src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py:
  - lines 712–716:
    "# Special case: for a decorated function, set the lineno to that of the
       # first decorator, not the `def`. Issue #4984.
       if isinstance(item, ast.FunctionDef) and item.decorator_list:
           lineno = item.decorator_list[0].lineno
       else:
           lineno = item.lineno"

- getstatementrange_ast is used by TracebackEntry.getsource (src/_pytest/_code/code.py lines 258–261) — so any change here affects traceback and assertion presentation.

3) Tests and coverage implications
- Reasoning: I inspected testing/code/test_source.py to see what behaviors are covered and whether trimming decorator lines could break existing tests.
- Findings:
  - The test suite has tests dealing with comments, blank lines, decorators, try/finally, multiline expressions, etc. There is an explicit test that checks Source of a decorated function still contains the decorator line(s) (test_source_with_decorator). However, that test uses Source(function) via inspect.getsource (not getstatementrange_ast for a frame lineno), so it would not be impacted by trimming decorators from the end of getstatementrange_ast ranges.
  - There's a number of tests that depend on precise getstatementrange values (e.g., try/finally/orelse cases) — these exercise the numeric offsets in get_statement_startend2, so changing those offsets is riskier.

4) Cross-check of claims in the draft Enhanced Report
- The draft's main claims:
  - getstatementrange_ast may include decorator lines from the next statement because it doesn't trim them. Verified: code trims comments/blanks but not decorators.
  - get_statement_startend2 uses hardcoded offsets (x.lineno - 1 and val[0].lineno - 1 - 1) and those numeric adjustments are sensitive to AST lineno semantics across versions. Verified: the code contains those exact expressions and they are indeed sensitive to AST semantics. The draft's assertion that these assumptions can interact with newer CPython AST semantics to cause the symptom is consistent with the code behavior and the example from the original bug report (Python 3.9 difference).

5) Missing or incomplete claims in the draft
- The draft omitted a useful corroborating observation: rewrite.py already special-cases decorated function lineno when inserting imports, which demonstrates pytest has had to account for decorator-line AST semantics in at least one other place. This is evidence that inconsistent handling of decorator/lineno semantics exists across the codebase and should be included as a related finding.
- The draft suggested the correct relationship in prose (that if the slice end points at a decorator line that line belongs to the next def/class and must not be included in the previous statement's snippet). That is diagnostic — I will preserve this phrasing in RootCause.

6) Completeness check (are there other locations sharing the same root-cause pattern?)
- I searched for other occurrences of the specific numeric arithmetic (lineno - 1 and the - 1 - 1 pattern) and for other uses of getstatementrange_ast. The only code that uses getstatementrange_ast is the code that converts a TracebackEntry to a source snippet (TracebackEntry.getsource) and Source.getstatement (consumer). The numeric offsets exist only in src/_pytest/_code/source.py. The only other place that handles decorator_list specially is src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py (workaround), which I will include in RootCause as a secondary finding demonstrating related assumptions and existing special-casing.

Summary of verified facts to include in the revised report
- The following lines in src/_pytest/_code/source.py are the primary evidence:
  - get_statement_startend2 line that appends x.lineno - 1 and val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 (see file lines ~150–158).
  - getstatementrange_ast does not trim decorator lines; it trims only comments/blank lines at the end (see lines ~205–211).
- src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py contains a special-case setting lineno to item.decorator_list[0].lineno when inserting imports (lines ~712–716) — this demonstrates decorator-handling is considered elsewhere in the project.

Phase 1 conclusion
- The draft's primary diagnostic claims are supported by the repository: getstatementrange_ast can include trailing decorator lines (because it doesn't trim them) and get_statement_startend2 uses hardcoded numeric adjustments that are sensitive to AST lineno semantics. I also found a related special-case in rewrite.py that shows decorator linenos are treated specially elsewhere.

I did not find other code locations that encode the same incorrect assumption beyond those listed above; the major consumer of getstatementrange_ast is the traceback/source formatting code (TracebackEntry.getsource and Frame.statement), so those are the affected call sites.

bug_report.Title: pytest prints next-function decorator line in assertion failure on Python 3.9
bug_report.Description: When an assert fails, pytest sometimes includes the decorator line(s) (e.g. '@t') of a subsequently-defined function in the displayed source snippet for the failing statement. This occurs on Python 3.9 (observed in the report) and results in an extra '@t' line appearing between the failing assert line and the assertion details/diff.
bug_report.RootCause:
1) src/_pytest/_code/source.py:getstatementrange_ast computes a statement slice by calling get_statement_startend2 to get candidate start/end indices, possibly expanding the range with BlockFinder/tokenize, and then trimming trailing blank/comment lines only. It does not trim trailing decorator lines (lines beginning with '@'). Because decorated FunctionDef/ClassDef AST line-number semantics changed in newer CPython (the lineno attributed to the node and/or decorators can move), the computed end index can end up such that the returned slice includes a decorator line that belongs to the next statement. The code that trims at the end only checks for comments/blank lines and therefore allows decorator lines to be returned and printed.

2) src/_pytest/_code/source.py:get_statement_startend2 converts AST lineno values into 0-based indices using explicit numeric arithmetic (x.lineno - 1) and, for 'finalbody'/'orelse', uses val[0].lineno - 1 - 1 (i.e. subtracting 2). These hardcoded offsets encode assumptions about how AST nodes advertise their lineno and about orelse/finally boundaries. Those adjustments are sensitive to AST lineno semantics across Python versions and therefore participate in the symptom where the computed end can reach decorator lines.

3) src/_pytest/assertion/rewrite.py contains a special-case that sets lineno = item.decorator_list[0].lineno for decorated FunctionDef items when inserting imports (i.e. rewrite.py already treats decorator-list lineno semantics specially elsewhere). This shows that decorator/lineno behavior has been handled inconsistently across the codebase and that special-casing exists in other locations.
StepsToReproduce: Create file test_statement.py with the following contents:
from pytest import fixture
def t(foo):
return foo
@fixture
def foo():
return 1
def test_right_statement(foo):
assert foo == (3 + 2) * (6 + 9)
@t
def inner():
return 2
assert 2 == inner
@t
def outer():
return 2

Run pytest with Python 3.9 (pytest 6.2.5 used in the report). Observe the failure output; note that a '@t' decorator line from the subsequent inner function is printed between the assertion line and the assertion failure details.
Repeat the same test with Python 3.7 to confirm the decorator line is not printed in that environment.
bug_report.ExpectedBehavior: When an assertion fails, pytest displays only the source lines belonging to the failing statement (and nearby context that legitimately belongs to that statement). It must not include decorator lines that belong to a subsequently-defined function or class.
bug_report.ObservedBehavior: On Python 3.9, the displayed snippet for a failing assertion can include one or more decorator lines (e.g. '@t') from the next function defined after the assertion. The decorator appears between the assertion source line and the assertion failure/diff, causing confusing output.
